proposals.title: Modeling Divergent Memory Trajectories in Amnesia: A Computational Simulation Using MINERVA 2 and Fuzzy-Trace Theory
proposals.motivation: Classical neuropsychological studies show that amnesic patients exhibit stark dissociations between impaired explicit memory and preserved implicit or semantic memory. However, the precise computational mechanisms driving these divergent trajectories remain underspecified. Grounding these dissociations in formal cognitive models would provide a mechanistic bridge between behavioral patterns and the neural systems compromised in conditions like PTSD and medial temporal lobe amnesia.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the MINERVA 2 model of episodic memory, when lesioned to reduce its echo intensity, will simulate the explicit memory deficits of amnesia, while a separate gist-based semantic network, informed by fuzzy-trace theory, will capture the relative preservation of semantic and implicit memory functions.
proposals.method: We will implement a computational framework with two core modules. First, a standard MINERVA 2 architecture will encode detailed event traces (verbatim memory). Second, a separate semantic network will extract and store gist-based representations, as per fuzzy-trace theory. We will simulate the 'amnesic' condition by degrading the strength of retrieval echoes in MINERVA 2, while leaving the semantic network intact. Model performance will be validated against classic datasets from amnesia research, testing recognition, semantic priming, and false memory (e.g., DRM) paradigms across simulated groups.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, testable model of memory dissociation, moving beyond descriptive accounts. It will clarify how damage to a hippocampal-like episodic system spares semantic and gist-based processes, with implications for understanding memory distortions in PTSD and the structure of semantic memory in aging. The model will generate novel predictions for future neuroimaging and behavioral studies.
proposals.novelty: While MINERVA 2 and fuzzy-trace theory have been applied separately, their integrated use to computationally lesion specific memory processes and simulate the full profile of amnesic deficits is novel. This approach directly connects Schacter's work on memory systems and false memory to a mechanistic, simulation-based framework.

proposals.title: Associative Binding and Network Dynamics: Differential Functional Connectivity of the Parahippocampal Cortex During High- and Low-Association Scene Processing
proposals.motivation: The parahippocampal cortex (PHC) is critical for processing contextual and associative information, particularly for scenes. However, pre-2026 neuroimaging work has primarily focused on PHC activation levels, leaving its dynamic functional connectivity with other memory regions (e.g., hippocampus, retrosplenial cortex, angular gyrus) during associative processing poorly understood. This gap limits our models of how distributed brain networks support the binding of elements into coherent memories, a process central to both accurate recollection and the formation of false memories.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that processing scenes with high semantic or contextual associations will engage stronger and more extensive functional connectivity between the PHC and a distributed network of medial temporal and parietal memory regions, compared to processing low-association scenes. This differential connectivity will specifically correlate with subsequent associative memory performance.
proposals.method: We will use high-resolution fMRI (3T) to scan participants during an incidental encoding task where they view scenes pre-rated for high or low inter-object associative strength. Functional connectivity will be assessed using psychophysiological interaction (PPI) analyses with bilateral PHC as seed regions. We will employ a multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) approach to decode associative strength from PHC activity patterns and use dynamic causal modeling (DCM) to infer the directionality of influence between the PHC, hippocampus, and angular gyrus during high- vs. low-association trials. A subsequent surprise associative memory test will link neural measures to behavior.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a detailed map of how the PHC interacts with its network partners to support associative binding, moving beyond simple localization. It will clarify the neural mechanisms underlying the continuum from strong, coherent memory formation to weaker, more error-prone associations. The findings will inform theoretical models of constructive memory and have implications for understanding associative memory deficits in conditions like PTSD, where contextual processing is disrupted.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies link PHC to context, this proposal uniquely focuses on its *dynamic connectivity profile* as a function of associative strength, integrating Schacter's work on memory construction and semantic distance. Applying connectivity and effective connectivity analyses to this specific cognitive contrast offers a novel, network-level perspective on a classic region's function.

proposals.title: Predictive Coding in Neural Networks: A Framework for Understanding Memory-Guided Attention and Simulation
proposals.motivation: Cognitive neuroscience has shifted from viewing the brain as a passive projector of stored representations to an active predictive engine, a framework central to understanding perception and memory. However, analogous computational models of neural networks often retain a 'mirror' or 'projector' metaphor, limiting our understanding of how these systems dynamically guide attention and generate simulations (e.g., future thinking, semantic creativity). This project bridges this gap by formally applying the predictive coding framework to network architectures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the internal dynamics of a neural network trained on sequential or structured data (e.g., language, events) are best characterized not as self-projection, but as a process of minimizing prediction error, where attention mechanisms are fundamentally driven by the discrepancy between the network's internal generative model and incoming input.
proposals.method: We will implement a deep recurrent neural network (e.g., an LSTM or Transformer encoder) with a dedicated prediction-error calculation module at each processing layer, inspired by predictive coding architectures. The network will be trained on narrative sequences or semantic association tasks. Using representational similarity analysis (RSA) and layer-wise activation tracking, we will test whether internal attention patterns (e.g., attention weights in a Transformer) correlate with the magnitude of contextually generated prediction errors, especially during tasks of future event simulation and false memory induction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational account of how memory systems support simulation and creativity by reframing network dynamics as predictive error minimization. It will generate testable neural correlates for Schacter's 'constructive episodic simulation hypothesis' and offer a formal model for how 'semantic distance' in creative ideation might emerge from iterative prediction-error reduction.
proposals.novelty: While predictive coding is established in neuroscience, its rigorous application as an explanatory framework for the internal attentional and representational dynamics of artificial neural networks, particularly in the domain of memory and simulation, is novel and non-obvious.

proposals.title: Dissociating the Default Mode Network via Inverse Task Design: An Internal Model Attention Framework
proposals.motivation: The default mode network (DMN) is consistently implicated in autobiographical planning, social cognition, and semantic processing, leading to dominant theories like 'self-projection.' However, these theories are descriptive and struggle to explain the network's activation across diverse, non-self-referential tasks. A pre-2026 challenge is to move beyond correlation to identify the core, domain-general computational process that unifies DMN function, which is critical for understanding its role in memory, creativity, and disorders like PTSD where internal model use is altered.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the DMN's fundamental operation is the attentional selection and manipulation of internal models (e.g., social scripts, physical schemas, event structures), and that its apparent unity reflects the common recruitment of this process, not narrative self-projection. This predicts that tasks demanding attention to distinct model classes will dissociate DMN sub-networks, while tasks requiring integration will co-activate them.
proposals.method: We will employ an inverse task design fMRI paradigm, creating conditions that selectively target attention to internal models of social interactions, physical mechanics, and episodic narratives, while rigorously minimizing explicit autobiographical projection. Using multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) and functional connectivity measures, we will test for dissociable neural patterns within the DMN. We will further apply psychophysiological interaction (PPI) analyses to identify how these sub-networks couple with the frontoparietal control network across conditions, probing the mechanism of internal model attention.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, componential account of DMN function, directly testing the internal model attention framework against the self-projection account. It will generate a novel task battery for fractionating DMN contributions to cognition. The findings will bridge Schacter's work on episodic simulation and semantic creativity by identifying a common neural process underlying flexible internal model use for both memory and future thought.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its use of inverse design to *dissociate* the DMN, rather than seeking tasks that activate it as a whole, and in its specific operationalization of 'internal model attention' as a testable alternative to broader constructs like self-projection or scene construction.

proposals.title: Dissociating the Core Relational Function of the Hippocampus from Episodic Narrative Demands: A Multi-Method Fingerprinting Approach
proposals.motivation: A central debate in memory neuroscience is whether the hippocampus supports a specific cognitive function (e.g., relational binding) or is merely recruited by tasks with high episodic or narrative demands. Resolving this is critical for understanding memory's architecture and disorders like PTSD, where hippocampal dysfunction is central. Current neuroimaging often conflates core function with task-specific processing, leaving this debate unresolved.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hippocampal-anterior temporal (HC-AT) network, identified by convergent co-activation across diverse tasks, supports a core function of flexible relational integration. This core function can be behaviorally and neurally dissociated from high-demand episodic narrative tasks, which rely more on a distinct cortical 'narrative network'.
proposals.method: First, we will conduct a meta-analytic co-activation network analysis (using Neurosynth or GingerALE) on pre-2026 fMRI data to identify a candidate HC-AT 'core network' active across semantic, future simulation, and memory tasks. Second, in healthy adults, we will test if individual differences in this network's resting-state fMRI connectivity (using seed-based correlation) predict performance on a minimalist relational integration task (e.g., transverse patterning) but not on a high-demand narrative construction task. Third, we will test for a double dissociation in patient groups (e.g., medial temporal lobe amnesia vs. frontal patients), predicting that HC damage impairs relational integration but spares narrative construction, while the reverse pattern holds for frontal damage.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a rigorous, multi-method test for a separable core hippocampal function, moving beyond descriptive task-demand accounts. It will offer a novel neural fingerprint for relational processing and clarify the mechanistic basis of memory deficits in clinical populations by isolating core computational impairments from secondary task effects.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its adversarial design, actively testing for dissociation rather than merely associating activity with a task domain. It uniquely combines cross-paradigm meta-analysis, individual-differences neuroimaging, and neuropsychological double dissociation within a single framework to isolate core function.

proposals.title: Dissociating Domain-General Control from Core Function in the Hippocampus: A Causal and Multivariate Investigation of Memory, Imagination, and Semantic Integration
proposals.motivation: Neuroimaging studies consistently show hippocampal overlap during episodic memory, future simulation, and semantic integration tasks, fueling theories of a shared core function like scene construction or narrative binding. However, this overlap may instead reflect domain-general cognitive control demands (e.g., maintaining complex, multi-element scenarios) common to these paradigms. This conflation impedes understanding of the hippocampus's true functional architecture and its role in disorders like PTSD, where memory and future thinking are disrupted.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that overlapping hippocampal activation in memory, future simulation, and semantic tasks is driven by shared, domain-general narrative scaffolding demands, not a domain-specific core process. Causal disruption of these overlapping zones will impair performance across all three tasks, while multivariate pattern analysis will reveal distinct, task-specific neural codes within the same region.
proposals.method: We will use fMRI to identify hippocampal overlap during carefully matched episodic recall, future event simulation, and semantic integration (creative ideation) tasks. We will then apply offline, neuronavigated transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) to the peak overlap coordinate to test causal necessity for task performance. Concurrently, we will employ multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA), specifically multi-voxel pattern classification and representational similarity analysis (RSA), to test for distinct, task-specific information patterns within the overlapping hippocampal region.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel causal and multivariate test of a major theoretical debate in cognitive neuroscience. It will advance methods for interpreting neuroimaging overlap by dissociating general control from specific content processing. The findings will refine models of hippocampal function with implications for understanding memory distortions and disrupted future thinking in clinical populations.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond descriptive conjunction analyses, this project uniquely combines causal perturbation (TMS) with high-sensitivity multivariate fMRI to directly test whether overlap signifies shared function or shared control, a critical but unresolved question in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Causal Control of Mnemonic Integration: A Real-Time fMRI Neurofeedback Test of the Default Mode Network as an Integrative Engine
proposals.motivation: The Default Mode Network (DMN) is consistently active during rest and self-referential thought, but its core function remains debated. Pre-2026 theories often frame it as a passive baseline or a system for episodic simulation, yet a compelling but untested hypothesis posits it actively integrates multi-scale information—episodic details, semantic schemas, and interoceptive states—to construct coherent memories and narratives. Testing this causal, integrative role is critical for understanding memory construction, its breakdown in conditions like PTSD, and the neural basis of creative thought.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the DMN functions as a causal engine for multi-scale information integration, such that experimental up-regulation of DMN coherence during a memory task will directly enhance the behavioral and neural coupling of episodic, semantic, and interoceptive information, beyond mere increases in recall or rest-related activity.
proposals.method: Participants will complete a narrative memory task with controlled internal (e.g., emotional valence, self-relevance) and external (e.g., perceptual distraction) demands while undergoing real-time fMRI. A closed-loop neurofeedback system will use a pre-trained classifier (e.g., a support vector machine or real-time connectivity decoder) to monitor DMN posterior cingulate cortex (PCC)-medial prefrontal cortex (mPFC) functional connectivity. Based on this signal, task parameters (e.g., distraction level, schema-cue specificity) will be dynamically adjusted in an 'adversarial' manner to challenge or support integration. Behavioral outcomes will include measures of semantic distance in memory narratives and false memory patterns, alongside neural multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) of integration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first causal evidence for or against the DMN's role as an active integrator, moving beyond correlational resting-state studies. It will directly inform models of memory construction, false memory formation, and the cognitive deficits observed when integration fails, such as in PTSD. The novel neurofeedback paradigm establishes a framework for testing network-level theories of cognition with precise experimental control.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its use of closed-loop, adversarial neurofeedback to manipulate DMN states *during* a complex cognitive task, creating a causal testbed that distinguishes active integration from passive baseline or simple episodic retrieval functions.

proposals.title: The Default Mode Network as a High-Entropy Integrative State: Implications for Memory and Future Simulation
proposals.motivation: The Default Mode Network (DMN) is consistently active during rest and self-referential thought, but its fundamental computational role remains debated. Prevailing models often treat it as a passive baseline or a task-negative system, failing to explain its critical role in complex, integrative functions like episodic memory, future simulation, and creative thought. Reconceptualizing its core function is essential for understanding the neural basis of conscious cognition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the DMN's intrinsic activity reflects a high-entropy, metastable neural state that facilitates broad, probabilistic sampling from semantic and episodic memory networks. This state serves as the brain's primary integrative engine, from which lower-entropy, task-specific network configurations (e.g., for focused attention) temporarily emerge.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing and newly collected resting-state and task fMRI data (including future simulation and memory recognition tasks) from healthy and clinical (e.g., PTSD) cohorts. Using methods from dynamical systems and information theory (e.g., entropy measures of BOLD signal complexity, metastability analysis of phase synchronization), we will quantify the DMN's entropy and integration. We will employ Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA) to test if DMN activity patterns during rest exhibit higher semantic distance, correlating with behavioral measures of creative ideation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework for DMN function, moving beyond the resting/task dichotomy. It will directly link DMN dynamics to memory integration and future simulation processes central to Schacter's research. The findings could offer new biomarkers for conditions like PTSD, where integrative thought and future simulation are impaired.
proposals.novelty: This proposal inverts the classic 'low-energy baseline' view of the DMN by applying a thermodynamic analogy of a high-entropy integrative state, a perspective not yet explored in cognitive neuroscience. It uniquely connects DMN entropy metrics to specific cognitive constructs like semantic distance and future simulation.

proposals.title: Dissecting the Integrative Core: A Task-Contrast Mapping Approach to Default Mode Network Sub-Functions in Memory and Cognition
proposals.motivation: The default mode network (DMN) is consistently implicated in internally-directed cognition, but its characterization as a monolithic 'task-negative' baseline has obscured its functional heterogeneity. Pre-2026 neuroimaging, including Schacter's work on future simulation and semantic memory, suggests the DMN supports distinct integrative processes like autobiographical planning, social cognition, and semantic integration. A precise, mechanistic model of how these sub-functions are neurally instantiated and dynamically configured is critically lacking, limiting our understanding of integrative failures in conditions like PTSD.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that contrasting different integrative cognitive tasks (e.g., future episodic simulation, theory-of-mind reasoning, semantic association generation) against each other and against carefully matched non-integrative control tasks will reveal dissociable, yet partially overlapping, spatiotemporal signatures within the DMN, reflecting its dynamic functional sub-architecture.
proposals.method: We will employ a within-subjects fMRI design with multiple task pairs. Each pair contrasts an integrative task (e.g, constructing a detailed future event) with a closely matched perceptual/rote control (e.g, describing a presented picture). Data will be analyzed using a multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) approach within DMN masks defined by independent component analysis (ICA) of resting-state data. We will also apply psychophysiological interaction (PPI) analyses to examine task-dependent functional connectivity changes between DMN sub-regions and other networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond the DMN as a uniform entity by providing a fine-grained, task-based functional atlas of its integrative sub-processes. It will establish a novel methodological framework for studying the DMN's active contributions to cognition. The findings will offer a neural model for understanding how integrative processes succeed or fail, with direct relevance to memory distortions and disorders of self-projection.
proposals.novelty: The proposal inverts the standard neuroimaging logic by using rigorous task contrasts to isolate DMN sub-functions from each other, rather than subtracting them all as a baseline. This directly tests the network's internal functional architecture through controlled manipulation, a significant advance over inferring function from resting-state correlations or gross task-deactivations.

proposals.title: Causal Cartography of Memory Networks: Testing the Necessity of Conjunction-Defined Hubs for Future Simulation and Semantic Integration
proposals.motivation: Cognitive neuroscience heavily relies on conjunction analyses in fMRI to infer functional specialization, such as identifying a common 'core network' for episodic future thinking and semantic memory. However, this correlational approach cannot establish necessity, analogous to pre-lesion neuroimaging of language. This creates a critical gap: we cannot distinguish brain regions that are merely commonly engaged from those that are functionally specialized and necessary for core cognitive operations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant subset of brain regions identified via conjunction analysis across episodic future simulation and semantic integration tasks will prove non-necessary when causally perturbed, revealing a dissociation between correlational engagement and functional necessity.
proposals.method: We will first conduct a meta-analytic conjunction (using tools like Neurosynth) to map regions consistently active during future simulation and semantic tasks from the literature. In a within-subjects design, participants will perform validated behavioral tasks (e.g., an episodic future thinking task and a semantic association task) while undergoing fMRI to confirm personal conjunction maps. We will then apply repetitive Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation (rTMS) to each identified conjunction hub (e.g., angular gyrus, ventromedial prefrontal cortex) in separate sessions, using vertex stimulation as a control site, and measure performance impairment on the same tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, causal test of the sufficiency assumption in conjunction-based cognitive neuroscience. It will generate a novel 'causal cartography' for memory and future simulation networks, clarifying which hubs are necessary. This directly informs theoretical models of constructive memory by distinguishing critical neural components from epiphenomenal correlates.
proposals.novelty: While TMS is used to test necessity, systematically targeting conjunction-defined hubs to challenge the interpretative logic of conjunction analysis itself is a novel, methodologically reflexive approach. It adapts the core logic of cognitive neuropsychology's lesion method to the domain of multi-task neuroimaging meta-analysis.

proposals.title: Causal Validation of a Domain-General Core Network: A Lesion-Based Investigation of Semantic, Social, and Moral Cognition
proposals.motivation: Neuroimaging meta-analyses have identified a 'core network' of brain regions consistently co-activated across diverse cognitive tasks, suggesting a domain-general system. However, functional co-activation alone cannot establish causality or define the network's true functional boundaries. A critical, pre-2026 question is whether this network supports integrated, high-level cognition across domains like semantic memory and social reasoning, or if it comprises functionally segregated modules.
proposals.hypothesis: Focal lesions to key nodes of the putative core network (e.g., anterior temporal lobe, medial prefrontal cortex, temporoparietal junction) will produce correlated deficits not in a single domain, but across multiple domains, including semantic memory, social cognition, and moral reasoning, thereby providing causal evidence for an integrated, domain-general system.
proposals.method: We will recruit patients with focal, chronic lesions (e.g., from stroke or resection) to pre-defined core network nodes, identified via pre-2026 meta-analytic maps (e.g., from Neurosynth). A comprehensive behavioral battery will assess semantic memory (e.g., category fluency, picture naming), social cognition (e.g., theory of mind tasks), and moral reasoning (e.g., moral dilemma judgments). Performance will be compared to matched healthy controls. Voxel-based lesion-symptom mapping (VLSM) using non-parametric permutation tests (e.g., NPM software) will identify brain regions where damage is significantly associated with multi-domain deficit profiles, testing for shared neural substrates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first causal, lesion-based test of the domain-generality hypothesis for the core network, moving beyond correlational neuroimaging evidence. It will clarify the functional architecture of high-level cognition by determining whether key regions support integrated or segregated processes. The findings will directly inform models of cognitive impairment in conditions like semantic dementia and traumatic brain injury.
proposals.novelty: While lesion studies have tested individual domains, systematically linking core network damage to a *coherent profile* of deficits across semantically distant cognitive domains is novel. This approach synergistically combines the causal logic of lesion mapping with the network-level hypotheses derived from large-scale functional meta-analyses.

proposals.title: The Core Network's Domain-Generality: A Meta-Analytic Test of Its Role in Semantic Memory and Moral Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 neuroimaging research has identified a 'core' or 'default' network (including medial prefrontal, posterior cingulate, and lateral parietal cortices) that is consistently active during episodic memory, future simulation, and theory of mind tasks, suggesting a domain-general function for self-projection. However, its proposed involvement in other key cognitive domains, such as semantic memory and moral reasoning, remains less systematically validated. A comprehensive meta-analysis is needed to test the network's true domain-generality, which is crucial for refining theoretical models of large-scale brain function and its breakdown in conditions like PTSD, where overgeneralized memory and moral injury are features.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the canonical core network will show consistent, domain-specific patterns of activation during semantic memory and moral reasoning tasks, but that these patterns will be spatially distinct from, yet functionally integrated with, its engagement during episodic future thinking.
proposals.method: We will conduct a large-scale, multi-domain activation likelihood estimation (ALE) meta-analysis using pre-2026 neuroimaging studies from databases like Neurosynth and PubMed. We will create separate ALE maps for studies of (1) semantic memory (e.g., conceptual retrieval, category fluency), (2) moral reasoning (e.g., personal dilemmas, fairness judgments), and (3) episodic future thinking (as a baseline). Statistical contrasts (e.g., conjunction and subtraction analyses) will be performed using GingerALE software to identify shared and unique neural substrates. We will further characterize the functional profiles of identified regions using Neurosynth's term-based decoding.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative synthesis mapping the core network's engagement across semantic and moral domains, directly testing theories of its domain-generality. It will clarify whether semantic and moral cognition recruit the network in its entirety or specific subsystems, informing models of cognitive specialization within large-scale networks. The results will generate novel predictions for clinical research, such as how semantic deficits or moral injury in PTSD might relate to alterations in this network.
proposals.novelty: While past meta-analyses have focused on the core network in episodic or social cognition, a direct, quantitative comparison of its recruitment across semantic, moral, and future-oriented domains is novel. The approach moves beyond cataloging activations to formally testing for domain-specificity versus generality within the same analytic framework.

proposals.title: Differential Task Decomposition of the Default and Hippocampal Networks During Episodic Future Simulation and Memory Retrieval
proposals.motivation: Neuroimaging studies of episodic memory and future simulation consistently identify broad, overlapping networks, including the default mode and medial temporal lobes. However, this coarse anatomical overlap obscures the functional architecture—the specific sub-regions that support shared constructive processes versus those uniquely engaged for remembering the past or simulating the future. A more precise, functionally decomposed model is needed to understand both adaptive memory functions and their dysregulation in conditions like PTSD, where future thinking is often impaired.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a differential contrast framework to fMRI data will decompose canonical memory/future simulation networks into a stable core of regions supporting shared constructive elaboration and distinct, condition-specific modules that differentiate episodic retrieval from episodic future thought.
proposals.method: We will adapt the logic of differential expression analysis to fMRI. Participants will complete well-matched episodic memory retrieval, episodic future simulation, and semantic control tasks during scanning. Using a general linear model, we will generate statistical contrast maps (e.g., Future vs. Memory, Future vs. Semantic, Memory vs. Semantic). These contrasts will be submitted to a conjunction analysis to identify a shared core network. Subsequently, multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) and psychophysiological interaction (PPI) analyses will characterize the unique functional contributions and connectivity profiles of the condition-specific sub-regions isolated by the contrasts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, functionally decomposed model of the episodic system, moving beyond anatomical labeling to define sub-regions by their task-contribution profiles. It will offer a new analytical framework for network refinement applicable to other cognitive domains and clarify the specific neural substrates whose alteration may underlie disrupted future simulation in PTSD.
proposals.novelty: While contrasts are standard in neuroimaging, this proposal systematically adapts the formal, condition-isolating logic of differential expression analysis from genomics to functionally decompose networks, rather than simply identifying activated areas. This approach is novel in its explicit aim to separate shared core from unique task-specific components within a network family.

proposals.title: Testing Causal Necessity in Memory Networks: Directed Perturbation Modeling of Semantic and Episodic Future Simulation
proposals.motivation: Neuroimaging studies, including our own, consistently show overlapping neural activity during episodic future simulation and semantic memory retrieval, particularly in the hippocampus and default mode network. However, this overlap reflects correlation, not causation. A critical gap exists in determining whether the directed influence from semantic to episodic systems is causally *necessary* for constructing coherent future events, or merely co-active. This limits our mechanistic understanding of memory-based cognition and related clinical conditions like PTSD, where future simulation is impaired.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the effective connectivity from a core semantic hub (e.g., anterior temporal lobe) to the hippocampal episodic system is a causally necessary pathway for generating detailed, coherent episodic future simulations, but not for general semantic retrieval.
proposals.method: We will use task-based fMRI where participants generate detailed future events and perform semantic control tasks. For each participant, we will construct a Dynamic Causal Model (DCM) of a predefined memory network, estimating directed connections between key nodes (e.g., hippocampus, vmPFC, ATL). We will then perform *in silico* perturbation analysis using the DCM framework, systematically 'lesioning' specific directed connections (e.g., ATL→hippocampus) and simulating the network's predicted response. By comparing simulated BOLD signals under lesioned vs. intact models to empirical data, we can formally test the necessity of each pathway for the future simulation task.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, model-based framework for moving beyond functional localization to testing causal necessity in human cognitive neuroscience. It will directly inform theories of how semantic knowledge is leveraged for episodic construction. Methodologically, it will establish a template for using DCM-based perturbation modeling to dissect network causality in other cognitive domains.
proposals.novelty: While DCM is used to infer effective connectivity, its standard application does not test causal necessity. Our innovation lies in integrating DCM with systematic *in silico* lesioning to formally test if a modeled connection is necessary for a cognitive function, a significant advance over comparing correlation strengths or using non-directional functional connectivity measures.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Tracking of Hippocampal-Cortical Circuit Maturation During Integrated Memory, Navigation, and Social Problem-Solving in Youth
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has largely studied memory systems in isolation using simplified tasks, creating a gap in understanding how the brain integrates multiple cognitive domains for adaptive, real-world behavior. This is particularly critical during development, when hippocampal-cortical circuits supporting episodic memory, semantic knowledge, and future simulation undergo protracted maturation. Understanding this integrative process is essential for explaining both typical cognitive development and vulnerabilities in conditions like PTSD, where memory integration may fail.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the developmental trajectory of integrated real-world cognition is driven by the maturation of distinct hippocampal-cortical circuits, with anterior hippocampal pathways supporting social-semantic integration and posterior hippocampal pathways supporting spatial-navigational integration, and that their increasing functional coupling enables complex, collaborative problem-solving.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 3-year longitudinal fMRI and MEG study of 120 children (ages 8-16). Participants will perform an immersive virtual reality task requiring collaborative navigation, item memory, and social reasoning to solve puzzles. We will use multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) to decode task states and dynamic causal modeling (DCM) to assess effective connectivity between hippocampal subfields (e.g., CA1, subiculum) and cortical targets (e.g., prefrontal cortex, retrosplenial cortex). Growth curve modeling will track developmental changes in neural patterns and connectivity strength.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first longitudinal map of how hippocampal-cortical circuits mature to support integrated cognition, moving beyond snapshots of isolated systems. It will establish normative developmental benchmarks for neural integration during ecologically valid behavior, offering a new framework for understanding atypical development and memory-related psychopathology where such integration is compromised.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in combining a longitudinal developmental design with a complex, immersive task that simultaneously probes multiple cognitive domains, allowing direct observation of how hippocampal subsystems learn to coordinate over time, rather than inferring integration from studies of isolated functions.

proposals.title: A Dimensional Neural Taxonomy of Spontaneous Thought: Linking Semantic and Emotional Features to Dynamic Brain Network Configurations
proposals.motivation: Current research on spontaneous thought, often termed 'mind-wandering,' relies on a simplistic dual-state (on-task vs. off-task) framework that obscures its rich, multidimensional nature. This is a critical gap, as the content and dynamics of spontaneous thought are central to creativity, future simulation, and maladaptive patterns in conditions like PTSD. A precise, feature-based neural model is needed to understand how specific thought dimensions, such as semantic distance and emotional valence, emerge from brain dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific, reportable features of spontaneous thought—including its semantic structure, emotional tone, and temporal dynamics—will be predicted by distinct, reconfigurable patterns of interaction among large-scale brain networks (e.g., default, executive, salience), rather than by a single 'mind-wandering' network state.
proposals.method: We will use experience sampling with probe-caught reporting during resting-state fMRI and a minimally constrained semantic generation task to collect fine-grained ratings of thought content (e.g., episodic/semantic, self-relevance), form (e.g., constraint), and affect. Concurrent multimodal data (fMRI and EEG) will be analyzed using multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) and dynamic functional connectivity to identify neural signatures. Machine learning models (e.g., support vector regression, recurrent neural networks) will be trained to predict continuous thought dimensions from time-varying brain network configurations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, data-driven neural taxonomy of spontaneous thought, moving the field beyond the ill-defined mind-wandering construct. It will directly link Schacter's work on semantic distance in creativity and emotional future simulation to their underlying neural mechanisms during unconstrained cognition, offering new targets for understanding cognitive disruptions in clinical populations.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior studies categorizing thought as merely 'mind-wandering,' this project uses a high-dimensional feature space derived from experience sampling to model thought, applying predictive machine learning to uncover how these features are dynamically instantiated in the brain.

proposals.title: Narrative Demand as a Unifying Cognitive Mode: Testing the Role of Temporal and Self-Relevant Integration in DMN-FPCN Coupling
proposals.motivation: The functional roles of the Default Mode Network (DMN) and Frontoparietal Control Network (FPCN) are typically dichotomized as supporting internal versus external cognition. However, pre-2026 neuroimaging consistently shows their co-activation during tasks like autobiographical planning and episodic future thinking, as well as during spontaneous thought. This overlap challenges the binary framework and suggests a more fundamental, demand-sensitive cognitive mode that remains poorly characterized.
proposals.hypothesis: The specific pattern of DMN-FPCN coupling reflects engagement of a 'narrative demand' mode, a task-independent cognitive state optimized for integrating temporally extended, self-relevant information. This mode is engaged whenever cognition requires binding events into a coherent, contextually rich structure, whether imposed by an external task or generated internally.
proposals.method: We will use fMRI to measure DMN-FPCN coupling (via psychophysiological interaction analysis) across three conditions: (1) a directed 'future simulation' task requiring narrative construction, (2) a semantic memory task requiring contextual integration of related concepts, and (3) undirected rest. We will quantify the 'narrative demand' of task outputs and spontaneous thought reports using pre-2026 natural language processing tools (e.g., Latent Semantic Analysis for semantic distance, and LIWC for self-referential and temporal language). We will test if a computational model combining temporal scope and self-relevance scores predicts the degree of DMN-FPCN coupling across all conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, computationally grounded framework that unifies observations of DMN-FPCN overlap across task and rest states. It will directly link a specific cognitive operation—narrative integration—to a canonical neural signature, moving beyond descriptive network dichotomies. For memory research, it will clarify how semantic and episodic processes interact during complex, future-oriented cognition.
proposals.novelty: It posits that the DMN-FPCN overlap is not a simple shared process or a switch, but the signature of a specific, quantifiable cognitive mode defined by informational structure, not task domain. This reframes the overlap from a paradox to a predictable outcome of processing narrative-like information.

proposals.title: The Default Network as a Semantic Memory Explorer: Neural Dynamics of Spontaneous Thought and Creative Association
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has established the default mode network (DMN) as active during undirected thought and mind-wandering, often framed as a task-negative state. However, its specific cognitive function remains debated. Drawing on Schacter's work linking the hippocampus to both episodic and semantic memory, and recent findings on semantic distance and creativity, this proposal posits that spontaneous thought is not merely a lack of focus, but a distinct, active cognitive mode crucial for memory integration and creative idea generation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that during experimentally induced mind-wandering, the DMN will exhibit dynamic, non-linear patterns of functional connectivity that facilitate the exploration of broad semantic memory networks, and that the strength of this DMN integration will predict the semantic distance and creative potential of subsequent reported thoughts.
proposals.method: We will use a validated sustained attention task with intermittent thought probes to capture periods of focused attention and mind-wandering. Concurrent fMRI data will be analyzed using dynamic functional connectivity (e.g., sliding window correlations) focused on DMN subsystems (e.g., medial temporal, dorsal medial prefrontal, core). A multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) approach will be used to decode semantic content from cortical patterns. We will apply graph theory metrics (e.g., modularity, participation coefficient) to quantify network integration and relate these neural measures to behavioral ratings of thought content (semantic distance, novelty) provided by participants post-scan.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a mechanistic, network-based account of how spontaneous thought supports memory and cognition, moving beyond a simple task-negative view. It will directly link dynamic DMN states to a key cognitive outcome—semantic memory exploration—thereby bridging Schacter's memory research with contemporary neuroscience of self-generated thought. The findings could inform understanding of cognitive states in clinical populations (e.g., PTSD, where intrusive thoughts and memory dysregulation are central).
proposals.novelty: While the DMN is known to be active at rest, this study uniquely tests its role as an active *explorer* of semantic memory space, using dynamic connectivity and neural decoding to directly correlate network states with the qualitative, creative properties of spontaneous thought, framed as the functional dual of focused task-based cognition.

proposals.title: Valence-Specific Neural Networks of Future Simulation: A Functional MRI Study of Positive and Negative Episodic Future Thought
proposals.motivation: Episodic future simulation is crucial for adaptive behavior, yet its neural architecture is often studied without differentiating emotional valence. Pre-2026 work, including Schacter's on emotional future simulations, suggests valence may critically modulate core simulation networks like the default mode network (DMN). This is clinically relevant, as maladaptive negative simulation is a hallmark of disorders like PTSD and depression, but a direct, systematic neural comparison of positive and negative simulations is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that while positive and negative future simulations will share core hippocampal-cortical simulation networks, they will differentially recruit valence-specific subsystems: positive simulations will preferentially engage reward-related regions (e.g., ventral striatum, vmPFC), whereas negative simulations will more strongly engage threat-processing and salience networks (e.g., amygdala, anterior insula).
proposals.method: We will use high-resolution fMRI (3T) to scan 60 healthy adults during a cued future simulation task. Participants will generate vivid, specific future events in response to verbal cues pre-rated for valence. Using a block design, we will contrast BOLD activity during positive, negative, and neutral simulation conditions. Analyses will employ whole-brain multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) to decode valence from neural activity patterns and psychophysiological interaction (PPI) analyses to test for valence-specific functional connectivity between the hippocampus and cortical targets.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed map of how emotional valence fractionates the core future simulation network. It will clarify the neural mechanisms underlying adaptive (positive) versus maladaptive (negative) prospection, offering a normative baseline against which to compare clinical populations like those with PTSD, where Schacter's group has longstanding interest.
proposals.novelty: While past fMRI studies note valence effects, this proposal systematically contrasts valence conditions within a rigorous future simulation paradigm, using connectivity and multivariate analyses to move beyond simple activation differences to identify distinct, integrated network states.

proposals.title: Generalizing the Sleep-Retrieval Interaction: A Multi-Modal Investigation of Episodic and Semantic Memory Consolidation
proposals.motivation: Prior research, including Schacter's work, has established that sleep and targeted memory reactivation (TMR) can modulate the consolidation and creative recombination of verbal-semantic information. However, it remains unknown whether this interaction effect is specific to semantic networks or represents a general principle of memory organization. Testing this with other memory types, such as visual scenes and personal episodic details, is crucial for building a unified theory of memory consolidation and its role in adaptive cognition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the facilitative effect of sleep and cued retrieval on memory reorganization and integration will generalize to visual and episodic memories, but that the nature of the reorganization (e.g., toward gist extraction vs. detail preservation) will differ systematically across memory types.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-session fMRI design with three parallel experiments using distinct materials: (1) word-pairs (semantic), (2) object-scene composites (visual), and (3) autobiographical future simulation scripts (personal episodic). For each, participants will encode paired associates, undergo a post-learning nap with auditory TMR (cueing one item from each pair), and complete a retrieval session. We will analyze memory performance, semantic/associative distance (using computational models like Word2Vec for verbal and visual feature vectors from pre-trained CNNs for scenes), and neural pattern similarity in hippocampal-cortical networks.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly test the boundary conditions of the sleep-retrieval interaction, determining if it is a domain-general mechanism. It will provide novel evidence on how different memory systems are transformed during offline periods, with implications for understanding memory distortion in healthy aging and conditions like PTSD, where intrusive memories often involve sensory and episodic details.
proposals.novelty: While TMR effects are well-documented for simple associations, applying this paradigm to complex visual scenes and self-generated episodic content to measure reorganization in representational space is a novel integration of Schacter's work on memory, simulation, and semantic distance.

proposals.title: Targeted Reconsolidation Interventions After Sleep: Correcting Gist-Based Memory Distortions
proposals.motivation: Sleep is known to promote memory consolidation, but it can also increase reliance on gist and semantic associations, leading to memory distortions and false recognition, particularly for related but non-presented items. This process is a potential mechanism for memory errors in clinical populations like PTSD, where intrusive, inferential memories are common. Pre-2026 research has focused on how sleep *causes* these changes, but not on whether post-sleep interventions can *correct* them.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a targeted memory reactivation (TMR) protocol, applied after a period of sleep, can selectively reduce false recognition of semantically related lures by destabilizing and updating the over-generalized memory trace, thereby increasing memory specificity.
proposals.method: We will use a modified DRM paradigm where participants encode word lists. After a 12-hour period including nocturnal sleep, they will complete a recognition test. In the critical intervention condition, before this test, they will undergo a cued reactivation procedure using sounds paired with the *original* studied items during encoding. We will compare this to control conditions (no reactivation, reactivation with neutral cues). Neural correlates will be assessed using fMRI, focusing on hippocampal pattern separation and cortical semantic network activity (e.g., anterior temporal lobe). Behavioral and neural data will be analyzed using mixed-effects models and multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first evidence that post-sleep memory distortions are malleable and can be targeted for correction, extending the concept of reconsolidation to the post-consolidation phase. It will bridge cognitive neuroscience with clinical memory modification research, offering a novel mechanistic framework for interventions aimed at reducing over-generalized, inferential memories in disorders like PTSD.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in testing an *inverse* approach: instead of studying how sleep causes distortion, we test a post-sleep intervention to reduce it. This directly applies Schacter's 'future simulation' and 'semantic distance' frameworks to a corrective, rather than descriptive, context.

proposals.title: Hippocampal Engagement in Implicit Probabilistic Sequence Learning: An Adversarial Dual-Task Paradigm
proposals.motivation: The hippocampus is traditionally linked to declarative memory, but emerging evidence suggests it may also contribute to learning complex, high-order regularities even without conscious awareness. This challenges the strict declarative/non-declarative dichotomy. Resolving this is critical for understanding memory systems in healthy cognition and in conditions like PTSD, where implicit trauma-related associations may form. Pre-2026 neuroimaging studies have hinted at hippocampal activity during implicit learning, but have struggled to fully rule out explicit contamination.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the hippocampus is necessary for acquiring complex, probabilistic temporal sequences under conditions of attentional occlusion that prevent the formation of reportable explicit knowledge. This hippocampal contribution will be dissociable from its role in supporting conscious memory.
proposals.method: We will employ an adversarial dual-task paradigm: a primary visual Serial Reaction Time Task (SRT) with high-order (e.g., 3rd-order) probabilistic sequences, concurrent with a demanding auditory 2-back working memory task to occlude awareness. We will use fMRI in healthy adults to measure hippocampal activity during learning, and compare behavioral learning curves in patients with focal hippocampal amnesia to matched controls. Pupillometry will index cognitive load. Crucially, post-experiment, we will administer a forced-choice sequence generation task and a structured verbal interview (adapted from the Process Dissociation Procedure) to objectively and subjectively quantify any explicit knowledge, allowing trial-by-trial classification of 'implicit-only' events for neural analysis.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a definitive, multi-method test of hippocampal necessity and engagement in implicit statistical learning. It will clarify the boundaries of non-declarative memory systems and offer a novel paradigm for studying implicit learning in isolation. The findings will have implications for theoretical models of memory and for understanding memory-related symptoms in clinical populations where explicit recollection is impaired.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the 'adversarial' design that actively prevents explicit knowledge formation during learning, rather than assessing it post-hoc, combined with the multi-method convergence of fMRI, patient lesion evidence, and pupillometry to isolate a purely implicit learning signal.

proposals.title: Dissociating Associative Binding from Conscious Recollection: A High-Resolution fMRI Pattern Similarity Investigation of the Hippocampus
proposals.motivation: A central debate in memory neuroscience concerns whether hippocampal activity, particularly pattern similarity between encoding and retrieval, specifically signals the subjective experience of conscious recollection or a more fundamental associative binding process. Pre-2026 neuroimaging studies often conflate these, as 'Remember' judgments typically involve strong binding. This project directly addresses this confound to clarify the core computational function of the hippocampus in human memory.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that hippocampal pattern similarity directly tracks the objective strength of associative binding and contextual reinstatement, and will therefore predict source memory accuracy even for items subjectively reported with familiarity ('Know') or guessing, dissociating it from neural signals that correlate uniquely with the 'Remember' experience.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-resolution (7T) fMRI associative memory task where pairs of objects are encoded in distinct spatial contexts. Associative strength will be manipulated via repetition and interference. At retrieval, participants will make item recognition judgments followed by Remember/Know/Guess and source (context) judgments. We will use multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) to calculate pattern similarity (e.g., Pearson correlation, Mahalanobis distance) between encoding and retrieval activation patterns within the hippocampus. Crucially, we will model pattern similarity as a function of both objective source accuracy and subjective report, using mixed-effects models to test for dissociations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a positive, falsifiable model of hippocampal function, specifying that it supports associative binding irrespective of conscious awareness. It will refine interpretations of hippocampal contributions to false memories, PTSD (where intrusive memories involve strong binding without voluntary recollection), and semantic memory, where binding may occur without episodic detail.
proposals.novelty: The novel approach is to experimentally decouple associative binding strength from subjective recollection by analyzing hippocampal pattern similarity for 'Know' and 'Guess' trials with accurate source memory, a condition largely unexamined in prior pattern similarity work which focuses on 'Remember' vs. 'Forgotten' contrasts.

proposals.title: Decoupling Hippocampal Memory Traces from Conscious Recollection: A 'Cognitive Filter' Approach Using Implicit Retrieval and Multivariate fMRI
proposals.motivation: Neuroimaging studies, including seminal work by Schacter and others, consistently link hippocampal activity to conscious recollection. However, this correlation conflates the neural trace of a memory with the cognitive operation of conscious reporting, analogous to the observer effect in physics. This conflation limits our understanding of whether the hippocampus supports memory traces that can be active without direct conscious access, a critical question for models of memory and conditions like PTSD where intrusive memories blur this line.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that hippocampal activity patterns associated with specific memory traces can be detected using indirect 'cognitive filters' prior to or in the absence of conscious recollection, and that this activity will predict subsequent memory performance on implicit tasks.
proposals.method: We will adapt a continuous recognition paradigm where studied items are presented subliminally (masked) or under divided attention, followed by implicit tasks (e.g., semantic judgment) that do not require explicit memory reports. Using high-resolution fMRI, we will employ multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) and representational similarity analysis (RSA) to decode hippocampal activity patterns for specific items during these 'filtered' conditions. We will compare these patterns to those during subsequent explicit recall trials, testing for shared neural representations. Bayesian model comparison will assess whether pre-conscious hippocampal patterns predict implicit behavioral outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological framework for isolating hippocampal memory trace activation from conscious retrieval processes. It will directly test and refine theoretical models of hippocampal function, particularly its role in implicit memory. The findings could offer new insights into memory distortions in clinical populations, where conscious access is disrupted.
proposals.novelty: The proposal innovatively applies a quantum measurement analogy to a core neuroscience problem, moving beyond correlation to experimentally decouple processes. It combines established implicit memory paradigms with state-of-the-art multivariate fMRI analysis in a novel way to target pre-conscious hippocampal states.

proposals.title: Causal Circuitry of Conscious Recollection: A Dynamic Causal Modeling Test of Hippocampal-to-Cortical Driving Influences
proposals.motivation: A central debate in memory neuroscience concerns the neural correlates of conscious recollection. While fMRI studies consistently link hippocampal activity to successful recollection, this correlation does not establish a causal mechanism. It remains unknown whether conscious retrieval depends on the specific pattern of directed, causal influences from the hippocampus to neocortical regions, or merely on co-activated regional BOLD signals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the subjective experience of conscious recollection is an emergent property of specific, directed causal influences from the hippocampus to neocortical regions (e.g., medial prefrontal or angular gyrus), rather than being directly indexed by the magnitude of hippocampal activity alone.
proposals.method: We will apply Dynamic Causal Modeling (DCM) for fMRI to data from a well-established memory recognition task (e.g., Remember/Know) that dissociates recollection from familiarity. Using the SPM12/DCM12 framework, we will construct and formally compare a set of biologically plausible network models of hippocampal-cortical interactions. These models will systematically vary in their effective connectivity parameters, specifically testing whether models where recollection trials are best explained by strong driving inputs from hippocampus to neocortex outperform models based on regional activity or backward cortical-to-hippocampal influences. Bayesian Model Selection will identify the most likely causal architecture.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, model-based test of a causal circuit hypothesis for conscious recollection, moving beyond descriptive correlations. It will directly inform theoretical models of hippocampal-neocortical interaction during memory retrieval and offer a novel analytical framework for studying altered recollection in conditions like PTSD, where such circuits may be disrupted.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in applying DCM—a method for inferring directed neural influences—to directly test whether the causal *architecture* of hippocampal-cortical circuits, rather than regional activity magnitude, is the critical determinant of conscious memory experience, a question not addressable with standard fMRI analyses.

proposals.title: Dissociating Hippocampal Contributions to Conscious Recollection and Semantic Retrieval: An fMRI and Behavioral Investigation
proposals.motivation: A dominant model in cognitive neuroscience posits that hippocampal fMRI activity during retrieval is a direct neural correlate of conscious recollection. However, pre-2026 work by Schacter and others shows the hippocampus supports both episodic and semantic memory, and is active during future simulation and creative ideation, processes not solely defined by conscious recollection of past events. This assumption may obscure the hippocampus's broader role in constructive memory processes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that hippocampal activity during memory retrieval will be significantly modulated by the semantic distance and constructive demands of the retrieval task, independent of self-reported conscious recollection. Specifically, greater hippocampal engagement will be observed for high-semantic-distance, constructive retrievals even when recollection confidence is low.
proposals.method: We will employ a modified recognition memory paradigm within an fMRI design. Participants will study word pairs and later complete a cued-recall test with a subsequent Remember/Know/Guess judgment. Crucially, we will integrate a semantic distance metric (using pre-trained word embeddings like GloVe) to quantify the associative relatedness of both studied and novel, creatively generated responses. We will use multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) and parametric modulation of fMRI data to test whether hippocampal activity patterns are better predicted by semantic distance and response type (studied vs. generated) than by subjective recollection reports.
proposals.contribution: This work will challenge a narrow interpretation of hippocampal function by providing evidence that its role extends beyond signaling conscious awareness to supporting flexible, semantically guided reconstruction. It will bridge Schacter's work on constructive memory, semantic cognition, and future simulation, offering a more nuanced framework for interpreting hippocampal fMRI signals in health and in conditions like PTSD, where recollection and construction may be dissociated.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in directly testing the dissociation between hippocampal activity and conscious recollection by manipulating semantic constructiveness as an independent variable, moving beyond correlational approaches that link the hippocampus solely to subjective experience.

proposals.title: Dissociating Competitive Interactions from Shared Resources in Episodic and Procedural Memory Systems: A Model-Based fMRI Approach
proposals.motivation: A central challenge in memory research is distinguishing true competitive or cooperative interactions between memory systems (e.g., episodic vs. procedural) from the confounding effects of domain-general resource competition (e.g., attention, executive control). Pre-2026 neuroimaging studies often struggle to separate these explanations, limiting our understanding of fundamental memory architecture. This confound is critical to resolve for theories of memory systems and for interpreting findings in clinical populations where resource competition is altered.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that multivariate neural activity patterns during concurrent episodic and procedural memory tasks will be better explained by computational models of specific interactive modes (e.g., mutual suppression, cooperation) than by additive models of independent system activity plus a shared resource competition component.
proposals.method: We will first use model-based fMRI to establish neural fingerprints for each system in isolation: an episodic source memory task and a procedural serial reaction time task. We will then implement dual-task paradigms combining these tasks. Trial-by-trial predictions of neural activity patterns (e.g., in hippocampus, striatum, and prefrontal cortex) will be generated from: 1) independent system models, 2) models with a shared resource competition parameter, and 3) models with direct competitive or cooperative interactions. Using multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) and representational similarity analysis (RSA), we will statistically compare observed brain activity to these model predictions to identify the best-fitting interaction structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological framework for isolating true memory system interactions, moving beyond descriptive dual-task interference. It will yield a formal, testable model of how episodic and procedural systems interact at a neural level. The findings will clarify fundamental memory architecture and inform interpretations of memory alterations in conditions like PTSD, where trauma memories may interact competitively with habitual processes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using computational models to generate explicit, testable neural predictions for different interaction types, directly pitting them against a shared resource model. This goes beyond prior work that infers competition from behavioral interference or univariate activation changes, which are ambiguous.

proposals.title: Competitive or Cooperative? Neural and Behavioral Dynamics of Memory System Interactions in Integrated Real-World Cognition
proposals.motivation: Theoretical models of memory, including the influential Multiple Memory Systems framework, have long posited largely independent systems (e.g., episodic, semantic). However, real-world cognition rarely isolates these systems; it integrates them. Pre-2026 research using artificial dual-tasks or isolated paradigms cannot reveal how these systems interact under the complex, concurrent demands of naturalistic behavior. Understanding these interactions—whether competitive, cooperative, or independent—is crucial for a complete theory of memory and its role in integrated cognition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that when episodic, semantic, spatial, and social memory systems are concurrently engaged in an integrated, ecologically valid task, they will exhibit context-dependent competitive suppression, as evidenced by reduced neural activity in one system when another is highly engaged, rather than operating in strict independence or showing simple additive effects.
proposals.method: We will design a virtual reality-based 'Urban Planning Task' where participants must learn a city's layout (spatial), remember specific past events at locations (episodic), apply knowledge of civic rules (semantic), and negotiate with virtual characters for resources (social). Using fMRI, we will employ multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) and psychophysiological interaction (PPI) analyses to decode and quantify the engagement and functional connectivity of core memory networks (e.g., hippocampal, perirhinal, prefrontal) during distinct, overlapping task phases. Behavioral performance and think-aloud protocols will provide convergent measures of system interaction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct evidence for or against the strict independence of memory systems under ecologically valid, integrated cognitive demands. It will establish a novel paradigm for studying memory system interactions and reveal the dynamic neural principles governing how multiple memory networks coordinate—or compete—to support real-world behavior.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond artificial dual-tasks to an integrated, multi-system paradigm represents a significant advance. Testing for competitive suppression, rather than mere co-activation, directly challenges a core tenet of the independence hypothesis in a novel, ecologically grounded context.

proposals.title: Longitudinal DCM Reveals a Developmental Shift in Hippocampal-Cortical Hierarchical Control Underlying Explicit Memory Maturation
proposals.motivation: The protracted development of explicit memory is well-documented, but its neural basis is understood in terms of regional activation, not evolving circuit dynamics. Pre-2026 neuroimaging shows structural maturation of hippocampal-cortical pathways, yet how the causal, directed influence of the hippocampus on cortical targets changes across development remains unknown. This gap prevents a mechanistic understanding of memory system development as conceptualized in Schacter's memory systems framework.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the maturation of explicit memory is characterized by a developmental shift from a predominantly reciprocal hippocampal-cortical dialogue to a state where the hippocampus exerts top-down, hierarchical causal control over prefrontal cortical regions, and that this shift correlates with improved memory performance and reduced susceptibility to false memory.
proposals.method: We will employ a longitudinal design, collecting task-based fMRI data (using explicit recognition and implicit priming tasks) from a cohort of children (ages 8-10) at three timepoints over six years. Effective connectivity will be quantified using Dynamic Causal Modeling (DCM) for fMRI, specifically applying bilinear DCM to model directed influences within an a priori network (hippocampus, ventrolateral PFC, dorsolateral PFC, and striatum). Model comparison (Bayesian Model Selection) will identify the winning connectivity architecture at each age, and parameters will be tested for longitudinal change and correlation with behavioral memory metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first computational model of the causal neural mechanics driving memory system development. It will directly test systems-level theories of memory development by quantifying how the balance of competition or cooperation between hippocampal and striatal circuits changes with age. The findings will offer a neurobiological benchmark for typical development, with implications for understanding developmental disorders affecting memory.
proposals.novelty: While DCM has been used in adults and cross-sectional developmental studies, its application to *longitudinal* fMRI data to model the *trajectory* of effective connectivity is novel. This moves beyond describing *where* activation occurs to model *how* the flow of information between memory-relevant nodes changes causally over time.

proposals.title: Modeling the Cognitive Niche: A Multivariate Lesion-Symptom Mapping Approach to Validate Hippocampal Necessity for Specific Memory Types
proposals.motivation: Despite decades of lesion studies, establishing the *necessary* role of the hippocampus in specific memory types (e.g., episodic vs. semantic) remains challenging. Traditional lesion-overlap maps are correlational and struggle with multivariate brain-behavior relationships. A more rigorous, model-based method is needed to quantify how a specific brain region's absence collapses the functional network supporting a cognitive task, moving beyond simple anatomical co-occurrence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a computational model trained on whole-brain fMRI activation patterns from healthy individuals during a memory task will show a systematic, quantitative collapse in predicted task suitability only when the modeled hippocampal contribution is removed, providing stronger evidence for its necessity than lesion location alone.
proposals.method: We will adapt Ecological Niche Modeling (ENM) algorithms, specifically MaxEnt (Maximum Entropy), to the cognitive domain. Using healthy fMRI data from tasks probing different memory types, we will train a model where voxel-wise activation patterns are 'environmental variables' and successful performance is the 'species presence.' This model defines the multivariate 'cognitive niche.' We will then input lesion masks from patients (e.g., with hippocampal amnesia, semantic dementia, PTSD) as 'unsuitable habitat' for the key hippocampal variable. The model will generate a predicted suitability score for each patient's preserved network.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative framework for validating brain region necessity, directly testing theoretical distinctions between memory systems. It will advance lesion-symptom mapping from descriptive overlap to predictive, model-based inference. The method can be generalized to other brain networks and cognitive domains, offering a new tool for cognitive neuroscience.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the formal cross-disciplinary analogy, applying a well-established ecological modeling algorithm (MaxEnt) to the problem of cognitive necessity. This moves beyond standard multivariate pattern analysis by explicitly modeling the *absence* of a key variable within a preserved system, a conceptual shift from typical fMRI decoding approaches.

proposals.title: Causal Validation of Hippocampal Subregion Contributions to Memory Domains via Lesion-Informed Multivariate Pattern Decoding
proposals.motivation: Neuroimaging studies, including Schacter's early work, have identified distributed hippocampal and cortical networks supporting different memory domains (e.g., episodic vs. semantic). However, MVPA and related methods primarily reveal correlational patterns of activity, leaving their causal necessity for specific memory functions unclear. Integrating lesion data with modern decoding techniques is a critical next step to move beyond mapping associations and toward testing the causal architecture of memory systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Focal hippocampal lesions will selectively impair the decoding accuracy of multivariate classifiers for the specific memory domain (e.g., episodic future simulation, semantic association) that the lesioned subregion purportedly supports, as defined by prior healthy-state MVPA models, thereby validating the causal necessity of those neural patterns.
proposals.method: We will first train linear support vector machine (SVM) and potentially ridge regression decoders on high-resolution fMRI data from healthy participants performing well-controlled episodic (e.g., future simulation) and semantic (e.g., category judgment) memory tasks. These decoders will be designed to distinguish memory-domain-specific patterns within hippocampal subregions (e.g., anterior vs. posterior) and their cortical networks. We will then apply these frozen, pre-trained decoders to fMRI data from patients with precisely characterized focal hippocampal lesions (e.g., from stroke or surgery), predicting that decoder accuracy will be selectively reduced for the domain linked to the damaged subregion.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative method for causally validating fMRI-derived neural representations, bridging the gap between human neuroimaging and lesion studies. It will offer specific, process-level evidence for the functional specialization of hippocampal subregions, moving beyond gross structural involvement to test theories of memory organization. The 'computational assay' approach could be generalized to other cognitive domains and clinical populations, such as PTSD, where memory network dysfunction is central.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in using pre-2026 MVPA decoders not merely as descriptive tools but as causal assays when applied to lesion data, directly testing the necessity of identified patterns. This fusion of machine learning-based neuroimaging with classic neuropsychology creates a powerful synergy not commonly employed in pre-2026 memory research.

proposals.title: Validating Hippocampal Necessity for Episodic and Semantic Memory via Convergent fMRI and Lesion Network Mapping
proposals.motivation: Neuroimaging studies in healthy individuals consistently show hippocampal engagement during both episodic and semantic memory tasks, but they cannot establish necessity. Classic patient lesion studies demonstrate necessity but are often limited by small, heterogeneous samples and imprecise anatomical mapping.
