A rigorous, pre-2026 convergence of these methods is critical to resolve debates about the hippocampus's specific role in different memory types, which has implications for understanding memory disorders and PTSD.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that brain regions showing consistent fMRI activation for specific memory processes (e.g., episodic recollection vs. semantic recognition) in healthy adults will correspond to the lesion sites that cause maximal impairment in those same processes in neurological patients. Specifically, we predict that lesions to the hippocampal network identified via fMRI for detailed episodic simulation will cause more severe deficits than lesions to networks for general semantic recognition.
proposals.method: We will first conduct a novel fMRI study in 50 healthy adults using tasks probing episodic future simulation, false memory, and semantic recognition. Activation maps will be generated using SPM12. We will then apply a pre-2026 lesion network mapping technique (e.g., using the BCBtoolkit) to structural MRI/CT scans from a large patient registry (e.g., >100 patients with focal lesions). For each patient, we will compute the functional connectivity between their lesion location and our healthy fMRI-derived memory networks. Behavioral memory scores from patients will be correlated with these network disconnection measures using multiple regression, controlling for total lesion volume.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, data-driven validation of the necessity of specific hippocampal circuits for distinct memory functions, moving beyond mere association. It will create a unified framework for interpreting neuroimaging and lesion data, offering a more precise model of memory organization. The findings will clarify the neural basis of memory distortions and inform rehabilitation targets for conditions like PTSD, where episodic simulation is impaired.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has compared groups of patients and healthy controls separately, this proposal innovatively uses healthy fMRI networks as a functional template to quantitatively predict the cognitive consequences of individual, naturally occurring lesions. This direct integration of task-based network topography with lesion symptomatology at the individual level is a novel methodological synthesis in cognitive neuroscience.

proposals.title: Adversarial Model Comparison Reveals Individualized Hippocampal Contributions to Implicit Probabilistic Learning
proposals.motivation: Despite early neuroimaging evidence linking the hippocampus to implicit memory, the precise computational role of the hippocampus in non-declarative learning remains fiercely debated. Competing theories—including reinforcement learning, statistical inference, and covert episodic binding—offer distinct explanations, but standard group-level analyses are confounded by model-misspecification, potentially averaging over distinct individual cognitive strategies. This study directly addresses this core conflict in memory systems neuroscience by adopting an adversarial, individual-differences framework.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the hippocampus supports implicit probabilistic learning, but its specific computational signature (e.g., prediction error, uncertainty, or associative binding) will vary systematically across individuals, aligning with their best-fitting behavioral model. This would reveal a heterogeneous, rather than monolithic, hippocampal function.
proposals.method: Stage 1: Participants complete an implicit serial reaction time/weather prediction task battery designed to produce dissociable behavioral patterns. Individual choice data will be fit with a suite of pre-registered, competing computational models (e.g., Q-learning, Hidden Markov Model inference, Hebbian associative models) using hierarchical Bayesian estimation (e.g., HDDM or Stan). Stage 2: For fMRI, trial-wise regressors (e.g., prediction error, belief update) from each participant's best-fitting model will be used in a model-based analysis of hippocampal BOLD activity, with the competing models' regressors included as adversarial controls in the same GLM.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first rigorous, individual-level test of competing hippocampal theories of implicit learning, moving beyond group averages. It will establish a methodological framework for adjudicating between theoretical models in cognitive neuroscience by prioritizing individual computational phenotyping, thereby reducing theoretical bias in neural interpretation.
proposals.novelty: The adversarial, model-agnostic design is novel; instead of testing one theory, we pit theories against each other within individuals. The two-stage pipeline uses the winning behavioral model to guide the neural analysis, ensuring neural signals are interpreted through a validated, individualized computational lens.

proposals.title: A Reinforcement Learning Account of the Default Mode Network as a Task-Agnostic Policy Generator for Episodic and Semantic Memory Integration
proposals.motivation: The default mode network (DMN) is consistently active during internally-focused cognition, including autobiographical memory, future simulation, and social reasoning, but its precise computational function remains debated. Pre-2026 theories often frame it as a passive 'baseline' or a system for memory retrieval, lacking a formal, mechanistic model for how it actively integrates diverse information to guide behavior. This proposal addresses this gap by applying reinforcement learning (RL) frameworks, which have successfully modeled goal-directed decision-making in other brain networks, to formalize the DMN's integrative role.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the DMN implements a hierarchical, model-based RL algorithm that uses a hippocampal-cortical generative model to simulate and evaluate abstract, long-horizon policies (e.g., social or autobiographical plans), with its activity patterns encoding policy values and simulated state transitions even in the absence of an immediate external task.
proposals.method: We will develop novel, partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP) RL models where states are latent, abstract situations derived from episodic and semantic memory. Using fMRI, we will collect data during traditional memory tasks (e.g., future simulation, false memory paradigms) and instructed rest periods involving spontaneous thought. We will fit the RL models to the neural data using representational similarity analysis and trial-by-trial model comparison, testing specific predictions that DMN (particularly medial prefrontal and posterior cingulate cortex) activity correlates with the value of simulated policies and that hippocampal activity patterns track transitions in the latent state space of the internal model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal computational mechanism for the DMN's active, integrative function, moving beyond descriptive correlational accounts. It will directly link RL frameworks, typically applied to externally-guided action, to the domain of internally-generated thought and planning, offering a unified theory for how memory systems support complex, goal-directed cognition.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the typical RL application by modeling task-agnostic, internal policy generation instead of external task performance, and in formally testing the hypothesis that 'resting' DMN activity reflects ongoing policy evaluation within a generative world model constructed from memory.

proposals.title: Graph-Theoretic Analysis of Hippocampal Network Reconfiguration Across Explicit and Implicit Memory States
proposals.motivation: A core distinction in memory research is between explicit (conscious) and implicit (unconscious) systems, yet their distinct neural architectures within the hippocampus remain incompletely characterized. Pre-2026 neuroimaging has identified regional activations but lacks a systems-level description of how the hippocampal network functionally reconfigures to support these different computations. This gap limits our understanding of memory's fundamental organization and its breakdown in conditions like PTSD, where implicit trauma memories intrude upon conscious states.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicit memory retrieval will be characterized by a more integrated, high-efficiency hippocampal network topology, while implicit memory processing will involve a more segregated, modular architecture, analogous to the functional reconfiguration of a road network between free-flowing and congested states.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing high-resolution fMRI or intracranial EEG datasets from participants performing well-established explicit (recall/recognition) and implicit (priming) memory tasks. For each condition and participant, we will construct functional connectivity graphs of hippocampal subfield and cortical node activity. Using the Brain Connectivity Toolbox, we will compute traffic-inspired metrics: betweenness centrality to identify critical hubs, global efficiency for integration, and modularity for segregation. Statistical comparisons of these graph metrics across memory conditions will test for network reconfiguration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, systems-level description of the distinct functional network states underlying explicit and implicit memory within the hippocampus. It will bridge cognitive theory (the multiple memory systems framework) with computational network neuroscience. Furthermore, it will establish a novel analytical framework for comparing pathological versus healthy memory network states, with clear relevance for disorders like PTSD.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct cross-disciplinary adaptation of quantitative urban traffic flow analytics to a fundamental neuroscience problem, moving beyond simple activation maps to a dynamic network architecture perspective on a classic memory dichotomy.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Network Reorganization of Hippocampal Systems Underlying the Differentiation of Explicit and Implicit Memory in Development
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Schacter's work, has established that explicit and implicit memory rely on distinct but interacting neural systems, with the hippocampus central to explicit memory. However, how these systems neurobiologically differentiate during childhood and adolescence remains poorly understood. A longitudinal, network-level analysis is critical to move beyond regional activation and reveal how large-scale functional integration and segregation support the development of these fundamental memory processes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the developmental differentiation of explicit and implicit memory systems will be characterized by a progressive increase in the modularity and small-world topology of hippocampal networks, with the hippocampus becoming a more specialized hub specifically for explicit, but not implicit, memory task networks across adolescence.
proposals.method: We will collect longitudinal fMRI data from a cohort of children (ages 8-10) over 5 years during well-established explicit (recognition) and implicit (priming) memory tasks. Functional connectivity matrices will be constructed for each task and timepoint. Using graph theory (implemented via the Brain Connectivity Toolbox), we will compute key topological metrics: modularity (to assess system segregation), betweenness centrality (to identify hub regions), and small-worldness (sigma). We will use mixed-effects models to track developmental trajectories of these network properties and relate them to behavioral performance gains.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal map of how large-scale hippocampal network topology reorganizes to support memory system specialization. It will directly link specific network properties (e.g., hub strength of the anterior hippocampus) to behavioral milestones in memory development. The findings will offer a mechanistic, network-based framework for understanding typical and atypical development of memory systems, with implications for conditions like developmental amnesia or PTSD.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined memory development or hippocampal networks in isolation, this proposal uniquely fuses a longitudinal design, network neuroscience methods, and a direct contrast of explicit/implicit memory tasks to reveal the *process* of system differentiation, moving beyond static snapshots or regional analyses.

proposals.title: Graph-Theoretic Signatures of Hippocampal Network Dynamics in Explicit and Implicit Memory Retrieval
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 neuroimaging has established the hippocampus as central to explicit memory, but its role in implicit memory remains debated, with some studies suggesting its involvement is condition-dependent. Traditional univariate fMRI analyses may obscure the network-level dynamics that differentiate these memory systems. Applying a graph-theoretic framework to functional connectivity data offers a powerful, systems-level approach to characterize how the hippocampus interacts with cortical networks during different memory states, potentially resolving long-standing theoretical conflicts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicit memory retrieval will be characterized by a more integrated, hub-like role of the hippocampus within a large-scale cortical network, while implicit memory retrieval will be associated with a more segregated, modular hippocampal connectivity profile, reflecting its more constrained or condition-specific functional coupling.
proposals.method: We will re-analyze existing and newly collected fMRI data from tasks dissociating explicit (recognition) and implicit (priming) memory. Functional connectivity matrices will be constructed from hippocampal subregion and cortical ROI time-series. Using the Brain Connectivity Toolbox, we will compute graph metrics (e.g., degree centrality, participation coefficient, modularity) to quantify network integration and segregation. Statistical comparisons of these metrics across memory conditions will test our hypothesis, with potential mediation analyses linking network properties to behavioral performance.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, quantitative network-based characterization of hippocampal function across memory systems, moving beyond simple activation maps. It will offer a mechanistic framework for understanding condition-specific hippocampal recruitment and could inform models of memory disruption in disorders like PTSD, where network dysregulation is a key feature.
proposals.novelty: While graph theory has been applied to resting-state and cognitive state networks, its targeted application to contrast the network architecture of explicit versus implicit memory retrieval within the same participants is novel. This approach directly tests systems-level predictions from Schacter's early neuroimaging work on the hippocampus.

proposals.title: Hippocampal Dynamics During Naturalistic Memory Encoding and Retrieval: An fMRI Study Using Continuous Narrative Stimuli
proposals.motivation: Traditional fMRI studies of memory often use isolated stimuli (e.g., words, pictures), which lack the temporal and semantic complexity of real-world experiences. This limits our understanding of how the hippocampus supports the formation and retrieval of coherent, episodic memories. Advancing to naturalistic paradigms, such as movie-watching, is critical for bridging the gap between controlled laboratory findings and the ecological validity of autobiographical memory, a core interest in Schacter's work on memory and future simulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that hippocampal activity during the viewing of a coherent narrative film will show stronger and more temporally structured patterns of reinstatement during subsequent recall, compared to a scrambled version of the same film, specifically tracking the retrieval of event boundaries and causal relationships.
proposals.method: Participants will undergo fMRI while viewing a 20-minute narrative film and a scrambled, non-narrative control version. During a subsequent cued recall task inside the scanner, they will verbally recount the film's events. We will use intersubject correlation (ISC) analysis to identify brain regions, including the hippocampus, with synchronized activity during encoding. For retrieval, a speech-to-text pipeline will segment recall transcripts, which will be modeled using word2vec or BERT embeddings to derive semantic similarity time series. These will be used as regressors in a parametric fMRI analysis to identify hippocampal regions where BOLD signal correlates with the retrieval of narrative structure.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel characterization of hippocampal function during the encoding and retrieval of complex, continuous events. It will directly test theories of episodic memory and future simulation in a more ecologically valid context. The findings will inform models of memory distortion and PTSD, where traumatic memories are often experienced as vivid, narrative replays.
proposals.novelty: While ISC has been used with movies, its combination with detailed, model-based analysis of free recall transcripts to quantify memory reinstatement in the hippocampus represents a significant methodological advance. This approach moves beyond simple subsequent memory effects to track how the narrative *structure* of a memory is neurally reinstated.

proposals.title: Linking Human Hippocampal fMRI Signals to Cellular Plasticity Mechanisms in a Rodent Model of Episodic Memory and Future Simulation
proposals.motivation: Human fMRI studies, including those by Schacter's lab, have identified hippocampal activity patterns critical for episodic memory and future simulation. However, the BOLD signal is an indirect, population-level measure, leaving the underlying cellular mechanisms—such as long-term potentiation (LTP) and ensemble reactivation—poorly understood. Bridging this gap is essential for developing mechanistic models of memory function and dysfunction, as seen in PTSD where maladaptive future simulations may arise from aberrant plasticity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific patterns of human hippocampal fMRI activity observed during episodic memory retrieval and future simulation tasks correspond to the reactivation and plasticity of distinct, spatially organized neuronal ensembles in the rodent hippocampus, governed by cellular mechanisms like NMDA-receptor-dependent LTP.
proposals.method: We will employ a cross-species translational design. In humans, we will use high-resolution (7T) fMRI during a well-established episodic memory and future simulation task to map hippocampal subfield activity. In parallel, transgenic mice (e.g., CaMKIIα-tTA;TetO-GCaMP6s) will perform a homologous spatial memory task in a virtual reality environment while undergoing in vivo 2-photon calcium imaging of hippocampal CA1 place cells. We will pharmacologically manipulate plasticity (e.g., with NMDA receptor antagonists) and use machine learning (specifically, representational similarity analysis) to identify shared neural representational geometries between human fMRI patterns and rodent cellular ensemble dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, mechanistic bridge between systems-level human cognitive neuroscience and cellular-level neurobiology of memory. It will directly link non-invasive biomarkers of memory function to specific plasticity mechanisms, offering new targets for understanding and treating conditions like PTSD where memory and future simulation are disrupted.
proposals.novelty: While prior work correlates fMRI with LTP-like effects in humans or studies plasticity in rodents, this proposal uniquely employs a direct, task-matched translational framework and advanced computational alignment to map human fMRI signals onto specific, imaged cellular ensembles and their plasticity states.

proposals.title: Decoding Mnemonic Success and Semantic Content from Hippocampal Activity Patterns: An Inverse Modeling Approach
proposals.motivation: Neuroimaging studies have traditionally mapped how memory tasks drive hippocampal activity. However, a critical inverse problem remains: can we reliably predict memory outcomes and content from hippocampal activity patterns alone? Solving this problem is essential for developing neural biomarkers for memory disorders like PTSD, where intrusive memories are linked to aberrant hippocampal function, and for understanding the neural basis of true and false memory formation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that multivariate patterns of hippocampal activity, measured during encoding and retrieval, can be used to decode (1) subsequent memory success (hit vs. miss) and (2) the broad semantic category of the remembered item, and that this decoding accuracy will be modulated by the semantic distance between encoded items.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing and newly collected fMRI data from episodic memory tasks. Using a within-subjects design, participants will encode and later recognize words from distinct semantic categories. We will apply multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) with linear support vector machines (SVMs) to decode memory success and category from hippocampal ROI activity. We will also employ representational similarity analysis (RSA) to examine how the neural representational geometry in the hippocampus relates to behavioral measures of semantic distance and false memory rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust framework for 'inverse' decoding of memory states from hippocampal signals, moving beyond descriptive mapping. It will directly link Schacter's work on semantic distance and creativity to memory neural codes, and provide a novel methodological approach for identifying neural signatures predictive of memory failures, with implications for clinical assessment.
proposals.novelty: While MVPA has been used in sensory cortex, its application to decode memory *outcomes* and semantic content specifically from the hippocampus represents a significant inversion of the standard forward inference approach. Integrating this with theoretical frameworks on semantic distance and false memory is a novel synthesis.

proposals.title: The Hippocampal Encoding-Forgetting Duality: A Neurocomputational Investigation of Active Forgetting as a Reversed Encoding Process
proposals.motivation: Memory research has traditionally focused on encoding and retrieval, treating forgetting as a passive failure. However, emerging pre-2026 evidence suggests forgetting can be an adaptive, goal-directed process. This proposal addresses a critical gap by investigating whether the neural mechanisms of intentional forgetting are the functional inverse of those for successful encoding, a 'dual problem' with implications for understanding memory optimization and disorders like PTSD where maladaptive memory persistence is central.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that hippocampal activation patterns during the successful encoding of subsequently remembered items will be the inverse (i.e., the functional dual) of hippocampal deactivation patterns during the intentional forgetting of subsequently forgotten items. Specifically, hippocampal subfield activity (e.g., in CA1 and DG/CA3) and associated cortical network connectivity will show opposing signatures.
proposals.method: We will employ a directed forgetting paradigm during high-resolution (7T) fMRI. Participants will encode word-pairs, followed by cues to 'Remember' or 'Forget'. Using multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA), we will train a classifier (e.g., linear SVM) to discriminate neural patterns during successful 'Remember' encoding trials. We will then test if this classifier inversely predicts patterns during successful 'Forget' trials. Functional connectivity (psychophysiological interaction analysis) between the hippocampus and prefrontal cortex (PFC) and default mode network (DMN) will be compared across conditions to identify network-level reversals.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct neural evidence for a computational duality between encoding and active forgetting within the hippocampus. It will advance theoretical models of memory by formalizing forgetting as an active neural operation. Furthermore, it will offer a novel framework for understanding clinical conditions characterized by failures of adaptive forgetting, such as the intrusive memories in PTSD.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links PFC-driven suppression to forgetting, the specific hypothesis that hippocampal forgetting patterns are the precise inverse of encoding patterns is novel and untested. This moves beyond correlational approaches to test a formal 'dual-process' model at the neural representational level.

proposals.title: Compensatory Hippocampal and Semantic Network Engagement Underlies Preserved Episodic Future Thought Fluency in Individuals with Suicidal Thoughts and Behaviors
proposals.motivation: Individuals with suicidal thoughts and behaviors (STBs) show reduced ventromedial prefrontal cortex (vmPFC) activation during episodic future thinking (EFT), a region critical for constructing vivid, detailed simulations. Paradoxically, behavioral fluency in generating future events is often preserved. This dissociation challenges neurocognitive models of EFT and may reflect a critical compensatory mechanism that maintains a core cognitive function despite emotional pathology, with implications for understanding cognitive resilience in high-risk clinical groups.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that preserved EFT fluency in individuals with STBs is supported by compensatory neural mechanisms, specifically increased recruitment of (a) the hippocampal formation for associative detail binding and (b) the anterior temporal lobe semantic network for accessing conceptual event knowledge, which offset the reduced integrative and evaluative functions of the vmPFC.
proposals.method: We will recruit three groups: individuals with STBs, a clinical control group with depression but no STBs, and healthy controls. Participants will complete a validated EFT fluency task during fMRI scanning. We will employ a multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) approach to decode neural patterns associated with semantic distance and episodic detail within generated events. Functional connectivity analyses (psychophysiological interaction, PPI) will test for increased hippocampal-anterior temporal lobe coupling during EFT in the STB group compared to controls, specifically during trials rated as high in fluency.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first neural evidence for compensatory mechanisms preserving EFT fluency in a high-risk clinical population, directly extending Schacter's work on adaptive constructive processes. It will refine neurocognitive models of future simulation by delineating how core subsystems can reorganize under vmPFC dysfunction. The findings could identify novel neural targets for interventions aimed at bolstering future-oriented cognition in suicide prevention.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has cataloged EFT deficits in depression, this proposal uniquely investigates a preserved function (fluency) and its underlying neural architecture in STBs, moving beyond a deficit-focused model. Integrating MVPA-based semantic distance decoding with clinical phenomenology offers a novel method to link neural pattern similarity to the qualitative content of future thoughts in psychopathology.

proposals.title: Hippocampal Functional Reorganization Without Overt Deficit: A Semantic and Future Simulation Framework
proposals.motivation: Neuroimaging studies, including Schacter's early work, show hippocampal engagement in memory, future simulation, and semantic processing. The dominant clinical and experimental model assumes that functional hippocampal differences (e.g., in PTSD or aging) must produce measurable behavioral memory deficits. This assumption may obscure subtler forms of hippocampal adaptation or reorganization that preserve performance on standard tests but alter the qualitative nature of memory and simulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that functional hippocampal differences, induced by experimental conditions or present in subclinical populations (e.g., trauma-exposed without PTSD), can reorganize memory-related networks to maintain overt behavioral performance while altering the semantic structure and emotional content of future simulations.
proposals.method: We will recruit trauma-exposed adults with and without PTSD, and matched controls. Using fMRI, we will assess hippocampal activity and functional connectivity during a validated future simulation task and a semantic relatedness judgment task. Behavioral performance will be measured via recognition memory and narrative detail counts. Crucially, we will apply computational semantic analysis (e.g., Latent Semantic Analysis, word2vec) to quantify the semantic distance and emotional valence of simulated events. We will use multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) and psychophysiological interaction (PPI) to identify network-level reorganization independent of gross behavioral deficit.
proposals.contribution: This work will challenge a fundamental assumption in memory neuroscience by providing a formal framework for identifying 'covert' hippocampal reorganization. It will bridge Schacter's work on future simulation, semantic memory, and false memory by showing how semantic structure underpins adaptive and maladaptive simulation. The findings could refine neurocognitive models of PTSD and resilience, where behavioral intactness may mask significant neural change.
proposals.novelty: The proposal moves beyond correlating neural activity with performance deficits, instead testing for a dissociation where neural reorganization is decoupled from overt behavioral impairment. It uniquely integrates computational semantic metrics with neuroimaging to quantify the latent, qualitative consequences of hippocampal functional differences.

proposals.title: Hippocampal Integrity and Adaptive Memory Processes as Markers of Resilience in Trauma-Exposed Individuals Without PTSD
proposals.motivation: Neuroimaging research has consistently linked smaller hippocampal volume and altered hippocampal function to Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD), often framing these as vulnerability factors or consequences of trauma. However, this deficit-focused perspective overlooks a critical inverse question: what are the neural and cognitive characteristics of trauma-exposed individuals who remain resilient? Understanding the markers of resilience is essential for developing a complete model of trauma response and could inform preventative interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that trauma-exposed, resilient individuals (without PTSD) will demonstrate preserved hippocampal volume and function, specifically characterized by enhanced pattern separation and coherent episodic future simulation, compared to trauma-exposed individuals with PTSD and non-trauma-exposed controls.
proposals.method: We will recruit three groups: trauma-exposed with PTSD, trauma-exposed without PTSD (resilient), and non-trauma-exposed controls. All participants will undergo structural and functional MRI. We will measure hippocampal volume via automated segmentation (FreeSurfer) and assess hippocampal function using two fMRI tasks: a mnemonic similarity task to probe pattern separation and an adapted episodic future simulation task where participants generate detailed personal future scenarios. We will analyze neural activity and connectivity patterns during these tasks, focusing on hippocampal networks and their coupling with prefrontal regions. Behavioral and clinical data will be correlated with neural measures.
proposals.contribution: This study will shift the focus from deficits to strengths, identifying specific hippocampal mechanisms associated with trauma resilience. It will contribute to Schacter's work on adaptive memory and future simulation by linking these processes to positive outcomes. The findings could refine neurobiological models of PTSD by delineating protective factors, potentially guiding early identification of at-risk individuals and targets for resilience-building therapies.
proposals.novelty: While most research compares PTSD patients to controls, directly contrasting trauma-exposed groups with and without PTSD to isolate resilience markers is less common. Furthermore, integrating assessments of pattern separation (a key implicit memory function) with episodic future simulation (an explicit, constructive process) provides a novel, multi-faceted examination of adaptive hippocampal function in resilience.

proposals.title: Differential Contributions of Medial Temporal Lobe Subregions to the Semantic Scaffolding of Future Simulation and False Memory
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Schacter and others has established that the parahippocampal cortex is critical for constructing future simulations and is implicated in false memories, likely by supporting the activation of associated semantic knowledge. However, the distinct roles of other medial temporal lobe (MTL) structures, particularly the entorhinal cortex—a primary gateway to the hippocampus—in these constructive processes remain poorly specified. Understanding this functional fractionation is crucial for developing a complete neural model of constructive memory and its failures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the entorhinal cortex will show a unique, non-linear relationship with the semantic distance of constructed content, acting as a key hub for integrating disparate semantic information during future simulation, and that its activity pattern will differentiate true from false memories more robustly than the parahippocampal cortex.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined fMRI and behavioral paradigm where participants generate future event simulations and later complete a recognition memory test, including lures of varying semantic distance from studied items. Using multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) and representational similarity analysis (RSA), we will quantify neural activity patterns in anatomically defined MTL subregions (entorhinal, perirhinal, parahippocampal cortices). We will model the relationship between neural pattern similarity and the quantified semantic distance (derived from pre-trained word embedding models like GloVe) of the generated and remembered content across conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a fine-grained map of MTL subregional contributions to semantic-based construction, directly testing theories of hierarchical processing within the MTL. It will clarify whether the entorhinal cortex serves as a critical 'semantic integrator' for simulation, offering mechanistic insights relevant to memory distortions in healthy aging and conditions like PTSD, where future thinking is impaired.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links the parahippocampal cortex to context and semantics, this proposal uniquely targets the entorhinal cortex's integrative role and employs a formal, computational metric of semantic distance to test for non-linear neural responses, moving beyond simple activation magnitudes to pattern-based representations.

proposals.title: Trauma-Related Semantic Processing and False Memory: A Scaled Behavioral and fMRI Paradigm
proposals.motivation: Prior work by Schacter and others has shown that semantic networks and future simulation are disrupted in PTSD, and that false memories can arise from semantic gist extraction. However, standard memory tasks using neutral stimuli may not capture the potent, schema-driven distortions characteristic of traumatic memory. A scaled, targeted paradigm using trauma-related words is needed to probe these specific mechanisms in clinical and subclinical populations, bridging cognitive neuroscience with clinical psychopathology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that exposure to trauma-related (vs. neutral) word lists will lead to a non-linear 'sweet spot' in semantic distance that maximizes both correct recognition and false alarms for related lures, and that this behavioral effect will be associated with altered hippocampal and default mode network activity during encoding and retrieval.
proposals.method: We will adapt the DRM paradigm and semantic distance tasks for fMRI. Participants (including a trauma-exposed group) will encode lists of semantically related words, with separate blocks for trauma-related (e.g., assault, crash, scream) and neutral (e.g., fruit, furniture) themes. Lists will be constructed using pre-2026 semantic vector models (e.g., GloVe) to quantify and vary semantic distance between studied words and critical lures. During scanning, participants will complete a recognition memory test. Analysis will use standard general linear models (SPM12 or FSL) to contrast BOLD activity for trauma vs. neutral conditions during successful encoding (subsequent memory effect) and during true vs. false recognition.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, efficient behavioral and neural assay for trauma-related memory distortions, linking the cognitive science of false memory directly to clinical features of PTSD. It will test whether principles of semantic network organization and hippocampal function, established with neutral materials, apply or break down under emotionally valenced, schema-relevant conditions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the 'semantic distance' framework and scaled behavioral tasks specifically to trauma-relevant stimuli within a neuroimaging context, moving beyond neutral laboratory paradigms to test cognitive theories in a clinically meaningful domain.

proposals.title: The Neural Sweet Spot: Linking Semantic Distance to Dynamic Network Connectivity During Creative Ideation
proposals.motivation: Recent behavioral work by Schacter and colleagues identified a 'sweet spot' of intermediate semantic distance as optimal for creative idea generation, a non-linear relationship. However, the neural mechanisms underlying this optimal cognitive state remain unknown. Understanding the brain network dynamics that facilitate this sweet spot is crucial for linking cognitive models of creativity to their biological implementation, with implications for enhancing creative thought and understanding its breakdown in clinical conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the behavioral sweet spot of intermediate semantic distance will be associated with a specific, transient pattern of brain connectivity, characterized by a balanced integration between the default mode network (DMN) and the executive control network (ECN). This pattern will differ from the connectivity states associated with overly close (low distance) or overly remote (high distance) semantic associations.
proposals.method: We will use a combined fMRI and semantic vector space approach. Participants will perform an Alternate Uses Task during scanning. Responses will be transcribed and their semantic distance from the cue object will be quantified using pre-2026 models like GloVe or BERT. We will employ time-resolved dynamic functional connectivity (dFC) analysis, using sliding-window correlations and k-means clustering to identify recurring brain states. We will then use multi-level modeling to test for non-linear (quadratic) associations between the moment-to-moment semantic distance of generated ideas and the probability of occupying specific dFC states.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first neural signature of the behavioral 'sweet spot' for creativity, moving beyond regional activation to characterize the dynamic network configurations that support optimal idea generation. It will bridge Schacter's work on semantic memory structure and future simulation with contemporary network neuroscience, offering a mechanistic account of how memory systems flexibly interact to produce creative thought.
proposals.novelty: While past neuroimaging studies have linked creativity to DMN or ECN activity, this proposal uniquely tests for a non-linear relationship between a quantifiable cognitive variable (semantic distance) and whole-brain network dynamics, directly translating a recent behavioral model into a testable neural hypothesis.

proposals.title: The Adaptive Side of Memory Distortion: How Misattribution and Bias Facilitate Creative Problem-Solving
proposals.motivation: Daniel Schacter's 'seven sins of memory' framework catalogs memory's failures, such as misattribution and bias, often studied for their maladaptive roles in false memory or PTSD. However, pre-2026 work on semantic cognition and creativity suggests that looser associative networks benefit creative thought. This proposal bridges these lines by testing whether these 'sins' are not merely bugs but adaptive features that directly enable creative cognition, a hypothesis with significant implications for understanding the functional architecture of memory.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific memory distortions, particularly semantic memory bias (the assimilation of past memories to current knowledge) and source misattribution, will be positively correlated with and causally contribute to performance on creative problem-solving tasks by enabling the flexible recombination of knowledge elements.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-method approach combining behavioral experiments and fMRI. Participants will complete established tasks to induce and measure memory bias and misattribution (e.g., DRM paradigm, source memory tasks). Creativity will be assessed using the Alternate Uses Task (AUT) and Remote Associates Test (RAT), with responses analyzed for semantic distance using computational semantic models (e.g., latent semantic analysis, word2vec). In a subsequent fMRI session, we will use a think-aloud protocol during creative problem-solving to identify neural correlates (e.g., hippocampal-cortical and default mode network connectivity) associated with both memory distortion tendencies and creative idea generation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct empirical link between specific memory 'sins' and creative cognition, moving beyond correlational studies of general memory function. It will offer a novel, integrative neurocognitive model positioning memory distortions as a mechanism for creative ideation, thereby reframing their functional role. The findings could inform interventions aimed at boosting creativity or understanding its deficits in clinical populations.
proposals.novelty: While memory's role in creativity is acknowledged, directly testing Schacter's specific 'sins' as facilitators of creativity is novel. The approach moves beyond viewing these distortions purely as errors, instead testing them as cognitive tools for generating novel associations, a non-obvious prediction from their typical characterization in the literature.

proposals.title: A Common Neural Substrate for Adaptive Memory Construction and Its Characteristic Errors: An fMRI Investigation of Semantic and Episodic Simulation
proposals.motivation: Schacter's adaptive constructive processes framework posits that memory's core function of flexibly constructing past and future scenarios inherently produces characteristic errors like false memories. However, the neural systems that simultaneously support both functional simulation and its errors remain poorly delineated. Identifying this shared neural architecture is crucial for a unified theory of adaptive memory, with implications for understanding disorders like PTSD where constructive processes become maladaptive.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a common core network, centered on the hippocampus and default mode network (DMN) subsystems, will show increased activity during both accurate episodic future simulation and the generation of semantically related but false memory lures, with the magnitude of activity correlating with behavioral measures of semantic distance and creativity.
proposals.method: We will use high-resolution fMRI (3T) while participants complete a modified future simulation task interleaved with a DRM-like false memory paradigm. Participants will imagine specific future events (episodic) and general future scenarios (semantic), followed by recognition tests inducing false memories. We will employ multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) and functional connectivity (psychophysiological interaction, PPI) to identify shared neural patterns across conditions. A key analysis will correlate neural pattern similarity in the hippocampus and angular gyrus with trial-by-trial semantic distance scores derived from word2vec or BERT embeddings.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct neural evidence for a shared constructive system underlying both adaptive memory use and its characteristic errors, bridging Schacter's work on memory, future simulation, and false memory. It will offer a mechanistic account of how semantic networks interact with episodic construction, informing models of cognitive creativity and clinical conditions involving memory distortion.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied accurate simulation and false memory separately, this proposal's novelty lies in directly testing their neural commonality within-subjects and linking neural activity to quantitative, trial-level metrics of semantic distance, a key predictor of creative ideation and memory errors.

ideas.title: Hippocampal pattern similarity signals associative binding, not conscious recollection per se
ideas.description: This project tests the specific hypothesis that hippocampal fMRI activity, particularly pattern similarity between encoding and retrieval, directly tracks the strength of associative binding and contextual reinstatement rather than the subjective experience of conscious recollection. We use a high-resolution fMRI memory task where associative strength is manipulated independently of later conscious report. The key analysis examines whether hippocampal pattern similarity predicts objective source memory accuracy and binding strength even for trials subjectively reported as 'Know' (familiarity) or 'Guess', dissociating it from activity that uniquely correlates with 'Remember' judgments. This provides a positive, falsifiable model for what hippocampal activity signals, moving beyond a simple critique.

ideas.title: Hippocampal activity equals conscious recollection
ideas.description: Relax the assumption that hippocampal fMRI activity directly signals conscious awareness during memory retrieval.
ideas.sourceTitle: Explicit Memory, Implicit Memory, and the Hippocampus: Insights From Early Neuroimaging Studies

ideas.title: Memory performance deficits are necessary
ideas.description: Relax the assumption that functional hippocampal differences must manifest in overt behavioral memory deficits.
ideas.sourceTitle: Hippocampal function in posttraumatic stress disorder

ideas.title: Scale up to naturalistic memory paradigms
ideas.description: Use movie-watching or narrative recall in fMRI to study hippocampal function in ecologically valid, complex memory.
ideas.sourceTitle: Explicit Memory, Implicit Memory, and the Hippocampus: Insights From Early Neuroimaging Studies

ideas.title: Inferring the Causal Drivers of Conscious Recollection via Dynamic Causal Modeling of Hippocampal-Cortical Circuits
ideas.description: This research direction synthesizes the relaxed assumption that hippocampal BOLD activity directly equals awareness with the analytical power of Dynamic Causal Modeling (DCM). The core hypothesis is that conscious recollection is an emergent property of specific, directed causal influences from the hippocampus to neocortical regions (e.g., medial prefrontal or posterior parietal cortex), not merely co-activation. By applying DCM to fMRI data from memory tasks, we can formally compare models where conscious recollection depends on the strength of hippocampal-to-cortical driving inputs versus models based on regional activity alone. This tests whether the causal architecture of hippocampal-cortical circuits, rather than hippocampal activity per se, is the critical neural correlate of conscious memory retrieval.

ideas.title: Dual problem: Study forgetting as active process
ideas.description: Investigate if hippocampal deactivation patterns during forgetting are the dual of activation patterns during successful encoding.
ideas.sourceTitle: Explicit Memory, Implicit Memory, and the Hippocampus: Insights From Early Neuroimaging Studies

ideas.title: Integrate neuroimaging with patient lesion studies
ideas.description: Combine fMRI data from healthy subjects with lesion maps to validate hippocampal necessity for specific memory types.
ideas.sourceTitle: Explicit Memory, Implicit Memory, and the Hippocampus: Insights From Early Neuroimaging Studies

ideas.title: The Narrative Demand Hypothesis: Reinterpreting the Default-Frontoparietal Overlap as a Task-Independent Cognitive Mode
ideas.description: This research direction posits that the observed activation overlap between the default mode network (DMN) and frontoparietal control network (FPCN) during both certain tasks and spontaneous thought does not reflect a shared core process or a simple duality. Instead, it reflects the engagement of a specific, demand-sensitive cognitive mode optimized for processing narrative-like, temporally extended, and self-relevant information structures, whether they are imposed by an external task or generated internally during mind-wandering. The core hypothesis is that this 'narrative demand' mode is a fundamental, task-independent organizing principle of cognition, whose neural signature is the specific pattern of DMN-FPCN coupling. The research would test this by identifying the precise computational demands (e.g., temporal binding, contextual integration) that predict this overlap across both directed and undirected cognitive states, challenging the traditional binary classification of brain network function.

ideas.title: Dual: study network during mind-wandering
ideas.description: Investigate the network during undirected, spontaneous thought as the dual of focused task-based cognition.
ideas.sourceTitle: Feeling-of-knowing in episodic memory: an event-related fMRI study

ideas.title: Validating Neural Necessity via Cross-Modal Decoders: Lesion-Informed MVPA for Domain-Specific Memory Networks
ideas.description: This research direction integrates lesion data with machine learning-based fMRI decoders to perform causal validation of neural representations. First, multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) is trained on healthy fMRI data to create classifiers that decode specific memory domains (e.g., episodic vs. semantic) from distributed hippocampal network activity. These decoders are then applied as 'computational assays' to fMRI data from patients with focal hippocampal lesions, testing the specific prediction that lesion location and extent will selectively disrupt the decoder's accuracy for the memory domain the lesioned subregion purportedly supports. The synergy lies in using the decoder's performance as a quantitative, domain-specific measure of functional disruption, directly testing the causal necessity of the patterned activity identified by the MVPA, rather than just the structure's gross involvement.

ideas.title: Identify neural correlates of adaptive construction
ideas.description: Use neuroimaging to find brain systems supporting both functional memory and its characteristic errors.
ideas.sourceTitle: Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences Memory: Sins and Virtues

ideas.title: Dissociable hippocampal contributions to attentionally-occluded probabilistic sequence learning: A multi-method test of non-declarative memory.
ideas.description: This study tests the hypothesis that the hippocampus supports the acquisition of complex temporal statistics even when learning is behaviorally implicit and inaccessible to conscious report. We employ a serial reaction time task with high-order, probabilistic sequences while participants simultaneously perform a demanding auditory working memory task, effectively occluding conscious awareness of the visual sequence regularities. Using a combination of fMRI in healthy adults, behavioral testing in patients with focal hippocampal amnesia, and pupillometry as a continuous index of cognitive load, we measure learning and neural engagement. The design's core strength is a rigorous post-experimental phase comprising a forced-choice generation task and a detailed verbal interview to objectively and subjectively quantify any explicit knowledge, allowing us to isolate hippocampal activity and necessity specifically for implicit learning trials.

ideas.title: Overlap as a marker of domain-general task control, not functional specialization
ideas.description: This fused idea proposes that activation overlap observed in neuroimaging studies is primarily a signature of shared, domain-general task control processes (like narrative scaffolding or goal maintenance) required by the experimental paradigms, not evidence for a shared, domain-specific core function. It further argues that traditional conjunction analyses, by only demonstrating common engagement, fail to prove that these overlapping regions are either necessary or sufficient for the hypothesized core process. The novel research direction is to design experiments that dissociate task-general control demands from putatively core functions, using causal methods (e.g., TMS, lesion studies) to test necessity, and multivariate pattern analysis to test for specificity within the overlapping zones. This moves beyond mere critique to a positive program for identifying the true functional architecture masked by overlap.

ideas.title: Use network neuroscience graph theory
ideas.description: Apply graph theory from network science to analyze hippocampal functional connectivity during explicit vs. implicit memory.
ideas.sourceTitle: Explicit Memory, Implicit Memory, and the Hippocampus: Insights From Early Neuroimaging Studies

ideas.title: Dual: study other medial temporal regions
ideas.description: Scale the investigation to other medial temporal lobe structures like entorhinal cortex, given parahippocampal findings.
ideas.sourceTitle: Hippocampal function in posttraumatic stress disorder

ideas.title: Causal Test of Default Mode Network as an Engine of Multi-Scale Information Integration
ideas.description: This research program empirically tests the hypothesis that the Default Mode Network (DMN) serves a primary integrative function, moving beyond the resting baseline analogy. It defines integration as the measurable coupling of episodic detail, semantic schema, and interoceptive signals, operationalized through a narrative memory task with controlled internal and external demands. A closed-loop neurofeedback system modulates task parameters based on real-time DMN activity, establishing a causal link between DMN states and behavioral/neural metrics of integration. This design yields specific, falsifiable predictions that can distinguish an integrative engine from a passive baseline state.

ideas.title: Lesion-Based Domain Validation of the Core Network
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the logic of lesion-symptom mapping with the goal of testing the core network across multiple cognitive domains. It proposes to systematically analyze patients with focal lesions to key network nodes, assessing for correlated deficits not just in one domain (like social cognition) but across several, including semantic memory and moral reasoning. This approach tests the hypothesis that the network functions as an integrated, domain-general system, as damage should cause a consistent profile of multi-domain impairment. The synergy lies in using causal lesion evidence to validate or refute the network's proposed domain boundaries derived from meta-analytic functional co-activation patterns.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Predict memory from hippocampal activity
ideas.description: Decode memory success or type from hippocampal activity patterns, inverting the standard approach of measuring activity from task.
ideas.sourceTitle: Explicit Memory, Implicit Memory, and the Hippocampus: Insights From Early Neuroimaging Studies

ideas.title: Dynamic Causal Modeling of the Maturation of Hippocampal-Cortical Circuits for Memory Systems
ideas.description: This research program integrates longitudinal neuroimaging with Dynamic Causal Modeling (DCM) to map the developmental trajectory of directed, effective connectivity within hippocampal-cortical networks supporting explicit and implicit memory. By applying DCM to task-based fMRI data collected from children through adolescence, we can quantify how the hippocampus's causal influence on regions like the prefrontal cortex and striatum evolves with age, and how these changing circuit dynamics relate to behavioral improvements in memory. This approach uniquely reveals whether the maturation of explicit memory systems involves a shift in hierarchical control or a change in the balance of competition between networks, providing a computational model of memory system development grounded in causal neural mechanics.

ideas.title: Task-Contrast Mapping of the Default Mode's Integrative Sub-Functions
ideas.description: This research direction inverts the standard task-baseline contrast. Instead of treating the default mode network (DMN) as a uniform baseline to be subtracted, it posits that the DMN's integrative function is its primary state, composed of distinct sub-processes (e.g., autobiographical planning, social cognition, semantic integration). By applying rigorous task-contrast methodology *within* the DMN—comparing different integrative tasks against each other and against non-integrative control tasks—we can isolate unique and shared neural sub-regions and time-courses that support these specific sub-functions. This reveals how the DMN dynamically reconfigures its internal functional architecture in response to cognitive demand, providing a mechanistic model of integration that is grounded in specific, observable task manipulations rather than inferred from rest alone.

ideas.title: Scale task to trauma-related stimuli
ideas.description: Use a scaled-down, more potent version of the memory task with trauma-related versus neutral words.
ideas.sourceTitle: Hippocampal function in posttraumatic stress disorder

ideas.title: Functional Dissociation via Lesion and Perturbation Studies
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that conjunction (overlap of activation across tasks or studies) proves a brain region is sufficient or specialized for a proposed core function. Drawing an analogy from cognitive neuropsychology, where the lesion method is used to move beyond correlational imaging data to establish necessity, we adapt this solution. In neuropsychology, observing that language tasks activate Broca's area does not prove it's necessary; only consistent deficits from lesions do. Similarly, in cognitive neuroscience, we propose a research program that systematically contrasts conjunction-based functional maps with maps generated from causal perturbation techniques (like TMS or optogenetics). By directly testing whether disrupting a region that consistently appears in conjunction analyses actually impairs task performance, we can dissociate regions that are merely commonly engaged from those that are functionally specialized and necessary. This 'causal cartography' approach formally challenges the sufficiency assumption inherent in interpreting conjunction analyses, forcing a more rigorous standard for claiming functional specialization.

ideas.title: Compare neural correlates of positive vs negative simulations
ideas.description: Directly contrast brain networks for simulating positive versus negative future events.
ideas.sourceTitle: Emotional future simulations: neural and cognitive perspectives.

ideas.title: Model-Based Dissociation of Memory System Interactions from Domain-General Resource Competition
ideas.description: This research directly addresses the confound of shared resources in studying memory system interactions. It first establishes neural fingerprints for episodic and procedural memory systems in isolation using model-based fMRI tasks. It then employs dual-task paradigms where computational models generate trial-by-trial predictions of neural activity patterns for both independent operation and specific interactive modes. By comparing observed multivariate brain activity to these model predictions, the design can statistically separate true competitive or cooperative interactions between memory systems from mere additive effects of competing for attention or executive resources.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Validate Brain Region Necessity
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from Ecological Niche Modeling to validate hippocampal necessity for specific memory types. In ecology, models predict species presence from environmental variables, and necessity is inferred from systematic absences where all but one key variable are suitable. Here, we computationally model the 'cognitive niche' of a memory task using healthy fMRI data, treating brain-wide activation patterns as the landscape. The model learns the multivariate pattern associated with successful task performance. We then input lesion maps from patients with memory deficits into this model. The adapted solution provides a rigorous, model-based validation: if the model's predicted 'suitability' for successful memory performance collapses specifically when the modeled hippocampal contribution is removed by the lesion—despite other contributing regions being preserved—it offers strong, quantitative evidence for the hippocampus's necessary role in that memory type, moving beyond simple lesion-overlap maps.

ideas.title: Individualized Computational Phenotyping to Disentangle Hippocampal Contributions to Implicit Learning
ideas.description: This adversarially improved study uses a model-agnostic, two-stage design to rigorously test competing hypotheses about hippocampal function. First, participants complete an implicit probabilistic learning task battery optimized to produce dissociable behavioral signatures under reinforcement learning, Bayesian inference, and episodic memory models. Individual behavioral data is fit with this suite of models to identify the best computational account per participant. In the second stage, trial-wise regressors derived from each participant's best-fitting model are used in a model-based fMRI analysis of hippocampal activity, ensuring neural signals are interpreted through a validated behavioral lens. This approach directly overcomes model-misspecification bias by letting individual behavior, not a pre-selected theory, guide the neurocomputational analysis.

ideas.title: Test sleep and retrieval on other memory types
ideas.description: Examine if the interaction effects generalize to other materials like visual scenes or personal events.
ideas.sourceTitle: Sleep and retrieval practice both strengthen and distort story recollection

ideas.title: Scale down to neural correlates
ideas.description: Link the behavioral sweet spot to specific patterns of brain connectivity during idea generation.
ideas.sourceTitle: A ‘Sweet Spot’ for Creative Ideation: Non‐Linear Associations Between Semantic Distance and Creativity

ideas.title: Ecological Competition: Testing Memory System Interactions in Integrated Real-World Tasks
ideas.description: This research program directly tests the hypothesis of strictly independent memory systems by designing and employing complex, ecologically valid tasks that simultaneously demand episodic memory, semantic memory, spatial navigation, and social reasoning. Using neuroimaging (fMRI) and behavioral measures, we will examine whether these systems exhibit competitive suppression, cooperative enhancement, or resource-sharing when forced to operate concurrently under real-world constraints. This moves beyond dual-task paradigms with artificial stimuli to reveal the dynamic, context-dependent interactions—or lack thereof—between memory networks as they support integrated cognition in naturalistic scenarios.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: resilience markers
ideas.description: Study the inverse: hippocampal function and volume in trauma-exposed individuals who did NOT develop PTSD.
ideas.sourceTitle: Hippocampal function in posttraumatic stress disorder

ideas.title: Adapting Urban Traffic Flow Analysis to Hippocampal Network Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from urban traffic flow analysis to the context of hippocampal network neuroscience. In traffic engineering, graph theory metrics like betweenness centrality, global efficiency, and modularity are used to compare how a city's road network functionally reconfigures under different conditions (e.g., rush hour vs. off-peak). We adapt this by applying the same analytical framework to fMRI or electrophysiological data of the hippocampus. By constructing and comparing functional connectivity graphs during explicit (conscious recall) and implicit (unconscious priming) memory tasks, we can quantify differences in network integration, segregation, and critical hub regions. This will reveal the distinct computational architectures the hippocampal network employs for different memory systems.

ideas.title: Reinforcement Learning Models of the DMN as an Integrative, Task-Agnostic Policy Generator
ideas.description: This research direction applies reinforcement learning (RL) modeling to formalize the hypothesis that the default mode network (DMN) performs an active, integrative function. We propose that the DMN utilizes a hippocampal-cortical circuit to maintain a generative model of the world—akin to an RL agent's internal model—which it uses to simulate and evaluate long-term, abstract policies (e.g., social or autobiographical planning) that are not tied to immediate external tasks. By fitting these novel RL models to fMRI data collected during both traditional tasks and putative 'rest', we can test specific predictions about how DMN activity patterns represent policy values and state transitions, thereby inverting the baseline assumption and providing a computational mechanism for its integrative role.

ideas.title: Test network in other cognitive domains
ideas.description: Extend the meta-analysis to other proposed domains of the core network, like semantic memory or moral reasoning.
ideas.sourceTitle: Feeling-of-knowing in episodic memory: an event-related fMRI study

ideas.title: Decoupling Hippocampal Activity from Conscious Recollection via a 'Cognitive Filter'
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that hippocampal fMRI activity directly signals conscious awareness during memory retrieval by drawing an analogy from quantum physics. In quantum measurement, the apparatus fundamentally alters the system being observed, analogous to how the act of conscious recollection may entangle with and alter the hippocampal memory trace. The analogous solution uses weak or indirect measurement to minimize this disturbance. Adapted to neuroscience, the research proposes to develop experimental 'cognitive filters'—such as subliminal priming, implicit retrieval tasks, or multivariate analysis of pre-conscious brain states—to obtain a purer measure of hippocampal memory trace activation that is not conflated with the subsequent cognitive operation of conscious reporting. This would test whether the hippocampus supports memory traces that can be active without direct conscious access, thereby refining models of hippocampal function.

ideas.title: Examine PHC connectivity during association processing
ideas.description: Map functional connectivity of PHC with other memory regions when processing high vs. low association scenes.
ideas.sourceTitle: Napping and the selective consolidation of negative aspects of scenes.

ideas.title: Investigate neural mechanisms of EFT fluency
ideas.description: Explore why EFT fluency is preserved despite reduced vmPFC activation in individuals with STBs.
ideas.sourceTitle: Ventromedial prefrontal cortex activation and neurofeedback modulation during episodic future thinking for individuals with suicidal thoughts and behaviors.

ideas.title: Link memory sins to creative cognition
ideas.description: Test if specific memory distortions, like bias or misattribution, directly facilitate creative problem-solving.
ideas.sourceTitle: Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences Memory: Sins and Virtues

ideas.title: Default Mode as a High-Entropy Integrative State
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumption that the default mode is a resting baseline by drawing an analogy from thermodynamics and statistical mechanics. In that field, the conceptual shift from viewing a system's 'ground state' as a simple, low-energy baseline to understanding the relevant biological baseline as a high-entropy, thermally populated ensemble of states provides a parallel solution. Adapting this, we propose the Default Mode Network is not a low-energy resting state but a high-entropy, metastable state that represents the brain's primary mode for integrative function. Its elevated activity facilitates a broad, probabilistic sampling of internal models, serving as the dominant operating point from which more focused, task-specific (lower-entropy) network configurations temporarily emerge. This reconceptualization frames the DMN not as an idle baseline but as the fundamental integrative engine of conscious cognition.

ideas.title: Scale down to cellular resolution in models
ideas.description: Use animal models to link human fMRI hippocampal signals to specific cellular plasticity mechanisms during memory tasks.
ideas.sourceTitle: Explicit Memory, Implicit Memory, and the Hippocampus: Insights From Early Neuroimaging Studies

ideas.title: The Network as a Predictive Engine, Not a Mirror
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that the network supports self-projection by drawing an analogy from cognitive neuroscience. The analogous field is the Cognitive Neuroscience of Perception, specifically the 'predictive coding' framework. This framework solved the analogous problem of explaining perceptual function by shifting from a view of the brain as a passive projector of internal maps to an active inference engine that constantly generates predictions and attends to prediction errors. The adapted research idea proposes that a neural network's fundamental process is not the projection of its internal state, but a basic, ongoing operation of attention directed at the mismatch (error) between its internal generative model and incoming sensory data. Its activity is therefore best understood as driven by the imperative to minimize this prediction error, reframing the network's role from a projector to a predictive engine engaged in continuous model refinement.

ideas.title: Developmental Neuroecology of Integrated Cognition: Tracking Hippocampal System Maturation During Real-World Task Performance
ideas.description: This research program longitudinally tracks children and adolescents using neuroimaging (e.g., fMRI, MEG) while they perform immersive, real-world tasks that simultaneously demand memory, navigation, and social reasoning, such as collaborative problem-solving in a virtual or physical environment. By scaling up task complexity to ecological validity within a developmental design, it uniquely reveals how the maturation of distinct hippocampal-cortical circuits enables the integration of cognitive domains over time. This approach moves beyond studying isolated systems or simple tasks to uncover the developmental mechanisms by which the brain learns to coordinate multiple memory systems for adaptive behavior in realistic scenarios.

ideas.title: Apply computational models of memory from cognitive science
ideas.description: Use models like MINERVA 2 or fuzzy-trace theory to simulate the divergent amnesic and control trajectories.
ideas.sourceTitle: Impaired implicit memory for gist information in amnesia

ideas.title: Dimensional Neural Mapping of Spontaneous Thought Dynamics
ideas.description: This project moves beyond the ill-defined concept of 'mind-wandering' to establish a dimensional neural taxonomy of spontaneous thought. Using experience sampling and probe-caught reporting during rest and minimally constrained tasks, it captures fine-grained features of thought content, form, and dynamics. Concurrent multimodal neuroimaging (fMRI/EEG) data is analyzed with machine learning to identify brain network configurations that predict these specific thought dimensions. The result is a robust, feature-based model of thought-brain relationships that replaces a simplistic dual-state framework with a high-dimensional, dynamic mapping.

ideas.title: Causal Necessity Testing via Directed Network Perturbation Modeling
ideas.description: This research direction integrates Dynamic Causal Modeling (DCM) with the critical insight that neural overlap indicates common usage but not causal necessity. The core innovation is to use DCM to estimate the directed effective connectivity between network nodes during a task, and then systematically model in silico 'perturbations' or 'lesions' to specific connections. By simulating the network's performance or dynamics with and without a particular directed influence, the approach can formally test if that causal pathway is necessary for the observed function, distinguishing it from merely co-active or sufficient regions. This provides a principled, model-based framework for establishing causal necessity in human neuroimaging, bridging a key gap between correlation and causation in cognitive neuroscience.

ideas.title: Developmental Network Neuroscience of Hippocampal Memory System Differentiation
ideas.description: This research program uses longitudinal neuroimaging and graph theory to track how the functional connectivity networks of the hippocampus and related structures change from childhood through adolescence. It will quantify developmental shifts in network topology—such as modularity, hub strength, and small-worldness—specifically during explicit and implicit memory tasks. This approach reveals not just when, but how the memory systems neurobiologically differentiate, linking specific network reorganization patterns to behavioral milestones in memory development.

ideas.title: Disentangling Shared Task Demands from Core Function via Network Decomposition
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that activation overlap in neuroimaging reflects a shared core cognitive function. Drawing an analogy from systems biology, where early protein interaction networks were confounded by indirect, condition-specific connections, we adapt their solution of network decomposition. In systems biology, methods like edge-betweenness were used to disentangle direct functional interactions from indirect ones caused by shared cellular contexts. Adapted to cognitive neuroscience, this involves modeling brain regions as nodes and their co-activation as edges, then applying graph-theoretical decomposition to separate a sparse, stable 'core' network (potential true shared function) from a dense, variable 'task-demand' network (reflecting common narrative processing, attention, or other paradigm-level demands). This provides a formal method to test whether observed overlaps are fundamental or epiphenomenal to task structure.

ideas.title: Inverse: Prevent distortion post-sleep
ideas.description: Test interventions after sleep to correct or reduce memory inferences.
ideas.sourceTitle: Sleep and retrieval practice both strengthen and distort story recollection

ideas.title: Refine network models via differential task decomposition
ideas.description: This idea refines brain network models by adapting the logic of differential expression analysis from functional genomics. In that field, contrasting gene expression profiles across conditions isolates condition-specific genes from a core set. Similarly, in neuroscience, by treating different cognitive tasks as contrasting conditions, we can apply statistical contrasts (e.g., Task A vs. Task B, Task A vs. Baseline, etc.) to neuroimaging data. This method will isolate shared, core sub-regions within a broad network that are consistently recruited across a task family from unique sub-regions that are selectively engaged for specific tasks. The outcome is a functionally decomposed network model that moves beyond coarse anatomical labeling to describe sub-regions defined by their distinct task-contribution profiles.

ideas.title: Dissociating the Internal Model Attention Network via Inverse Task Design
ideas.description: This research direction combines the theoretical challenge to the 'self-projection' account of the default mode network (DMN) with an inverse problem experimental approach. It proposes designing and employing tasks that selectively demand attention to distinct classes of internal models (e.g., schematic social scripts, physical laws, episodic event structures) while minimizing narrative self-projection. By analyzing which tasks dissociate activity within the DMN into separable sub-networks and which tasks co-activate them, the research can identify if the network's core function is a domain-general process of internal model attention. This provides a concrete pathway to move beyond descriptive correlation and toward a mechanistic, componential understanding of the network's fundamental operations.

ideas.title: Convergent Dissociation of Core Function from Task Demands via Cross-Paradigm Fingerprinting and Neuropsychological Dissociation
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond simply proposing task demands as an explanation to actively testing for a separable core function. It first establishes a candidate neural signature by identifying regions that co-activate across a wide array of tasks and contexts via meta-analysis. This candidate 'core network' is then stress-tested by examining whether its functional integrity, measured via resting-state connectivity or structural integrity, predicts performance on tasks stripped of superficial narrative or episodic demands. Finally, causal necessity is established by testing for double dissociations in patient populations, where damage to the candidate network impairs a hypothesized core computation (e.g., relational integration) but spares performance on high-demand narrative tasks that rely on other intact regions.

ideas.title: Millisecond-Scale Model-Based Tracking of Hippocampal Prediction Error Dynamics
ideas.description: This research direction combines reinforcement learning (RL) modeling with high-temporal-resolution neuroimaging (MEG/EEG) to track the millisecond-scale neural dynamics of implicit probabilistic learning.
